short_description: First, realistic PvP brawling game for up to 8 vs 8 players with original, dynamic combat system and fast, round based battles. Create and develop Your character. Earn new skills and items that helps You beat Your opponents. Gather Your friends and fight together in a clan.
detailed_description_raw:
This is the first, realistic PvP brawling game for up to 8 vs 8 players with original, dynamic combat system and fast, round based battles.
Create and develop Your character while earning new skills and items that helps You beat Your opponents.
Gather Your friends and fight together in a clan.

Compete with players from around the world using hand-to-hand combat.
  • No shooting or magic but a hail of swift blows of kicks and punches !
  • Easy, one-click matchmaking finds the best suitable battle for Your character.

Use dynamic combat system with intuitive action keys.
  • In Street Warriors Online You can dynamically attack or block incoming attacks as opposed to mmo games.
  • You can provide standard, special or sprint attacks that causes different hit reactions of Your enemies.

Take part in battles up to 8 vs 8 participants in 3 different game modes.
  • Fight on many battle arenas of different player capacities ( 2v2, 4v4, 8v8 )
  • Select one of 3 game modes:
    - Skirmish: Beat up all enemies to win a round. Win three rounds to win the battle !
    - Conquest: Capture bases by spraying over Your team's graffiti. Captured base generates points. Reach points limit to win.
    - Capture The Bag: Capture the bag and deliver it to Your van. Reach bags limit to win the battle.

Gain experience and respect to develop Your characters.
  • Each finished battle gives You Experience as well as Respect Points reward.
  • The reward is proportional to Your involvement in the battle.
  • Respect points is Your primary currency. Buy different items such as clothes, power cards or new attacks.
  • Level Up to obtain attribute points and develop character to match Your play style.
  • Higher levels unlocks new items and attacks !

Configure appearance and combat style to make Your characters become unique !
  • Compose Your appearance from over 150 clothing parts of 7 categories per character class.
  • Prepare preferable attack sequence ( rotation ) that suits Your combat tactics.
  • Spend Your attribute points to achieve best fighting results.
  • Buy and socket different Power Cards that alters Your combat skills.

Own up to 8 characters with different class and attributes build.
  • Get new characters for golden currency or from the golden premium bundle.
  • There are 3 different character classes to choose from: Swarmer, Slugger and Bruiser
  • Swarmer is a female class with extended speed and energy regeneration
  • Bruiser is a slow heavy fighter with increased damage and health
  • Slugger is the most balanced class

Use elements of the environment as weapons to gain temporary advantage.
  • On almost every arena You can find different items that can be used as weapons.
  • You can throw heavy objects like garbage bins or tires to hit many enemies at once and make them fall.
  • There are also objects like baseball bats, pipes and planks that You can use as one handed weapons.
  • Throw bricks, stones and bottles at Your enemies to stun them and make vulnerable to Your attacks.

Use energy drinks to scramble out from critical situations or get temporary power-up.
  • There are 8 different energy drinks so far.
  • You can take 2 different drinks on a battle and trigger them when desired.
  • If You have more drinks of a kind - drink will be available again after You re-spawn.

Team up with Your friends and play together !
  • Play together with Your friends in up to 8-person group on free for all battles.
  • You can create and customize Your unique clan of warriors and fight against others to climb up in the global rankings !
  • Higher clan ranking enables all members to buy special, unique rewards.

reviews:
['Street Warriors Online, developed and published by Crazy Rocks Studios, is an online multiplayer brawler that aims to capture the intensity of street-level combat within a competitive, team-based environment. Instead of relying on fantasy abilities or ranged weapons, it builds its identity around close-quarters fighting, where punches, kicks, and environmental interactions take center stage. The concept is immediately appealing—large groups of players clashing in chaotic urban arenas—but the experience ultimately depends on how well its systems support that ambition.

The combat system is designed to feel direct and physical, emphasizing timing, positioning, and awareness over complex ability rotations. Players can perform a range of attacks, including basic strikes, sprint-based moves, and heavier actions, while also blocking or countering incoming blows. This creates the foundation for a system that rewards player skill rather than automation, encouraging careful engagement rather than reckless button-mashing. When the mechanics work as intended, fights can feel dynamic and reactive, with players weaving between offense and defense in quick succession. However, the consistency of this system can vary, and at times the combat lacks the fluidity needed to fully deliver on its promise.

Matches take place across several multiplayer modes, ranging from straightforward team battles to objective-based formats that require coordination and map control. Larger matches, particularly those involving multiple players on each side, highlight the game’s chaotic energy, as fights break out across different parts of the map simultaneously. The inclusion of a clan system adds another layer of structure, giving players the opportunity to form groups and compete collectively. In theory, these elements create a strong social framework, but their effectiveness is closely tied to the availability of active players.

Progression is handled through a combination of leveling, stat allocation, and equipment customization. As players advance, they can improve their character’s attributes, unlock new moves, and equip items that influence combat performance. This system provides a sense of growth and long-term investment, but it also introduces balance concerns. Players with more experience or better gear can gain noticeable advantages, which may make early matches feel uneven for newcomers. While customization options are extensive, allowing players to personalize their appearance, the impact of progression on gameplay can sometimes overshadow the skill-based foundation the game aims to establish.

One of the more interesting aspects of Street Warriors Online is its use of environmental interaction. Many arenas feature objects that can be picked up and used during combat, adding an element of unpredictability to encounters. These improvised weapons can turn the tide of a fight or create unexpected moments of advantage, reinforcing the game’s emphasis on adaptability. However, this feature, like others, is not always fully refined, and its impact can feel inconsistent depending on the situation.

Presentation is where the game shows its limitations most clearly. Visually, it lacks the polish and detail expected from more modern titles, with environments and character models that feel dated. Animations, particularly during combat, can appear stiff, which detracts from the sense of impact that is crucial for a brawler. Sound design does little to compensate, offering minimal enhancement to the overall atmosphere. While these issues do not make the game unplayable, they contribute to an experience that feels less immersive than it could be.

A more significant challenge lies in player activity. As a multiplayer-focused game, its core experience depends on having a consistent player base. In practice, this can be difficult to maintain, and finding matches may not always be reliable. Without active participation, many of the game’s systems—team modes, clan interactions, and competitive elements—lose their relevance, leaving the experience feeling incomplete. This reliance on player engagement highlights one of the risks of online-only design, particularly for smaller titles.

Ultimately, Street Warriors Online is a game built on a solid concept that struggles to fully realize its potential. Its focus on melee combat and team-based encounters offers a refreshing alternative to more conventional multiplayer games, and there are moments where its systems come together to create genuinely engaging fights. However, technical limitations, balance concerns, and inconsistent player activity prevent it from reaching a higher level of polish and sustainability. It remains a niche experience that may appeal to players looking for something different, but it falls short of delivering a consistently compelling online brawler.

short_description: PUBLISHER UPDATE - This is the product page for The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion Game of the Year Edition, released in 2009. ---The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion® Game of the Year Edition presents one of the best RPGs of all time like never before. Step inside the most richly detailed and vibrant game-world ever created.
detailed_description_raw:
PUBLISHER UPDATE - This is the product page for The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion Game of the Year Edition, released in 2009.

---

The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion® Game of the Year Edition presents one of the best RPGs of all time like never before. Step inside the most richly detailed and vibrant game-world ever created. With a powerful combination of freeform gameplay and unprecedented graphics, you can unravel the main quest at your own pace or explore the vast world and find your own challenges.

Also included in the Game of the Year edition are Knights of the Nine and the Shivering Isles expansion, adding new and unique quests and content to the already massive world of Oblivion. See why critics called Oblivion the Best Game of 2006.

Key features:

  • Live Another Life in Another World

    Create and play any character you can imagine, from the noble warrior to the sinister assassin to the wizened sorcerer.

  • First Person Melee and Magic

    An all-new combat and magic system brings first person role-playing to a new level of intensity where you feel every blow.

  • Radiant AI

    This groundbreaking AI system gives Oblivion's characters full 24/7 schedules and the ability to make their own choices based on the world around them. Non-player characters eat, sleep, and complete goals all on their own.

  • New Lands to Explore

    In the Shivering Isles expansion, see a world created in Sheogorath's own image, one divided between Mania and Dementia and unlike anything you've experienced in Oblivion.

  • Challenging new foes

    Battle the denizens of Shivering Isles, a land filled with hideous insects, Flesh Atronachs, skeletal Shambles, amphibious Grummites, and many more.

  • Begin a New Faction

    The Knights of the Nine have long been disbanded. Reclaim their former glory as you traverse the far reaches of Cyrodill across an epic quest line.

New Lands to Explore In the Shivering Isles expansion, see a world created in Sheogorath's own image, one divided between Mania and Dementia and unlike anything you've experienced in Oblivion. Challenging new foes Battle the denizens of Shivering Isles, a land filled with hideous insects, Flesh Atronachs, skeletal Shambles, amphibious Grummites, and many more. Begin a New Faction The Knights of the Nine have long been disbanded. Reclaim their former glory as you traverse the far reaches of Cyrodill across an epic quest line.

['Such Beauty, I cry with my eyes closed on under the starry night sky and just think aout how beautiful and surreal this piece of ♥♥♥♥ art of a game is and what it does to me, what it does to my heart..', 'this game has great mechanics like durability on weapons and gear, a great magic system, completely "balanced"  gameplay and magic creation system but the game wont start if you have too good components, the game refused to start and just crashed every time i started it on max resolution, might just me a file conflict but point was given', 'A timeless classic.', '"They did a whole powerpoint presentation on everything that Obsidian did wrong and made us watch it, and we were like 'Wow... okay?'"', 'OF COURSE WE ALL KNOWW OF COURSE WE ALL SEEEEEEE ITS TIME TO END THIS MISERYYYY!!!', 'i cant put this game down lol', 'Good game. But why no controller support in 2026? 

I want to play this on my steamdeck without using keyboard binds.', "There once was a boy from Indooroopilly,
Who enjoyed putting cigarettes out on his willy.
Ethan's his name,
But don't put him to blame,
He was born a little bit silly.", 'Great Story, Great Gameplay & Great Memories. 

Shivering Isles is still the best DLC in gaming in my opinion.', 'By the gods, there’s a psychopath on the loose!', 'No controller support for PC version. I was excited to sit back and enjoy with a controller like I did with Skyrim, but sadly its M&K only. bummer.', "there's something so timeless about this game, and it reflects on the current state of the industry. the moment you step out, you're greeted by this lush greenery. the vibrant colors envelop you into a world of fantasy; it just opens to you. the scenery is whimsical and full of life. in the distance, a ruined temple. the gentle wind, and music as it enchants you.", 'It just works', 'very fun but level 10+ is kicking my ass time for skyrim again', 'cool
', 'People are lying to themselves when they say they like this game', 'Amazing and unintentionally funny.

Memes aside i wish some of the mechanics from Oblivion had made it into Skyrim like for example the Spellmaking, being able to cast spells without needing to holster your weapons first, better unarmed combat, attacks being independent of your movement (you can jump-attack here and swing your melee underwater - good luck doing that in Skyrim)

Writing and quests are better in Oblivion too.
(Tho the voice acting makes it feel like a Monty Python sketch)', 'fun', 'I played this game forever ago - when I was still in college. Some of the old quirks are still there, but I loved and still love the game mechanics where the repetition of using a skill dictates which abilities can be be increased when leveling up.', 'Is good.', 'Played on linux with Proton. It worked well most of the time. Had a couple of crashes here an there, but it ran very stable. The only this that didn't worked properly, is the "opening credits" before the start menu, where is says Bethesda and etc, you can't skiṕ it, and it will say that the steam_app stopped responding. The solution is just to let it run until you get to the main menu. In my case I always needed to alt+tab back into it. Only issue I had so far. Got 70+ hours of gameplay for just a couple of bucks. And I didn't even finished all of the side quests. Great value, but get it in a sale.', 'Game crashed twice within the first 3 hours of play.   
Controls and movement are very janky. 
Not a good experience.', 'Oblivion has a lot of oblivion in it', "Still broken, but it's kinda fun?", 'Man, I really love Oblivion. So it makes me sad to say that I just can´t recommend it.

This review will go over two things:
-Why I love Oblivion and want to play more of it
&
-Why I can´t recommend it to anyone, despite being so good

Why I like it:
-Oblivion is such an amazing game and there is so much to like about. Cyrodill is not only one of the most beautiful game worlds I have ever seen. 
-The game is littered with some of the funniest NPCs in gaming (heck, they were the main reason I bought the game). 
-Suprisingly, the questlines in Oblivion are very engaging, offering a lot of variety and fun. 
-Oblivions leveling system feels rewarding (It´s not as bad as people say). Passively leveling up your attributes by doing various things in the world.
-I even had a fun time with the combat (though its a bit simplistic and the enemies are damage-sponges).

I love it, but I can´t recommend it.
-One word: crashes
-I experienced more game crashes the longer I played Oblivion. Now 31 hours in, I CAN´T EVEN START THE GAME. Everytime I try to access my save file it crashes. 

It actually makes me sad because I really enjoyed my time Oblivion. Worst thing for me is that I won´t be able to experience most of the games content (WHICH IS A LOT).

But I won´t abandon Oblivion. One day (when I have an actual gaming PC), I will return to the world of CyrodilI. And shut the jaws of Oblivion...

Conclusion: Love it, but wouldn´t recommend it.', 'absolute masterpiece', 'Steal a guys shop right beneath his nose.', 'I get more action in Oblivion than I do at home. 10/10', 'Best game award! 10/10', 'Oblivion my beloved']

short_description: Play more of your favorite arcade hits from yesteryear. Includes SONSON for free!
detailed_description_raw:
Capcom Arcade 2nd Stadium: Mini-Album


Wax nostalgic with a selection of 16 of the best songs from titles included in Capcom Arcade 2nd Stadium!

Capcom Arcade 2nd Stadium: Mini-Album features a variety of tunes from some of Capcom's most beloved arcade classics, including Mega Man: The Power Battle and Street Fighter Alpha 3!


About the Game

Play more of your favorite arcade hits from yesteryear.
Includes SONSON for free!

Capcom is taking you back to the stadium with another collection of your favorite classic hits!
Come see what's changed, and what's completely new, in Capcom Arcade 2nd Stadium!

Just Like the Good Old Days
From 3D-rendered arcade cabinets to scanline filters, there's everything you need to recreate that arcade atmosphere. Fully customizable display settings let you craft your own personal experience and truly relive the glory days of arcade gaming.

Brand New Ways to Play
With gameplay rewind, speed adjustment, and the ability to save and load your game at any time, your old favorites will feel new all over again!
Every game has online leaderboard rankings, so you can see where you stack up against players all over the world!
Capcom Arcade 2nd Stadium, where retro appeal meets cutting-edge features for a fresh take on Capcom's classics!

Play hard. Dominate the scoreboards.
Track your progress across each game, including play count, times cleared, and personal high scores! Beat your bests, or those of your friends--there's always something to shoot/strive for!

Included Title
SonSon
Players: 1-2 Co-Op
Genre: Shooting
Versions: Japanese & English

reviews:
['this and the first one are the greatest collection of classic arcade games ever assembled.  highly recommend.', "A group of awesome heroes is waiting for your help. They're ready to take on some bad guys and need your skills to help them jump higher or throw balls more accurately. You know, the usual stuff. You can turn your own place into a real adventurer's den. Every corner will be filled with music and the sounds of victory. It's like having a museum of heroes in your own home. It's like stepping into a world where knights and planes come to life, telling stories of their battles. And you can join in the fun. Let's go on an epic adventure", "This is so bad, controler doesn't work, menues are japaneas style, tons of menues, 10 windows opens before you play one game, ps2 controler doesn't work, another trash form capcop.", ' .', 'well cat nice', "bought the DLC on sale, it's just great with nostalgia feeling, dun tell me there're many web emulators to perform the same games easily, it's an authorised combo, alright? the bad thing is the original key binding for each game sucks, and you have to rebind your preferences ones for the games one-by-one.", 'cap-pyt normalik 2d game piksel ff', 'The best game ever created!!! 

Nah... just memories.', "It used to work fine, now it doesn't load up.  Tried uninstalling and reinstalling but no joy.  There is no easy way to contact Capcom to ask for help.  I'll try it on another computer and see if it runs there.", 'When first looking at the underwhelming reception from the first Capcom Arcade Stadium both on Steam and Backloggd, i was like "well, of course. this collection is so lacking in features, how did they think this was good enough for the general public?" only to then find out the backlash was 90% due to a concerning amount of window lickers whining at the concept of exchanging money for food and services, who somehow thought the whole shebang was gonna be free. 
Either way, structurally Stadium 2 is exactly the same as the first one, and you can read all of my gripes with that one in its dedicated page. Haven't played the games in here yet, but from the looks of it they seem a lot better than the prequel's at least.', '', "Some games are too boring, Not worth for the price.I would rather play retroarch for sure! C'mon capcom, you can do better then that! 💀", 'Just like the first one, I wish we got more free games on it. Still good fun but.', "If you grew up pumping quarters into arcade cabinets, this collection is a nostalgia goldmine — games like Black Tiger, Knights of the Round, and Super Puzzle Fighter II Turbo alone make it worth picking up, especially on sale. The emulation is solid with modern conveniences like save states, rewind, and scanline filters, though the frontend feels bloated and sluggish for what are ultimately small retro games. My biggest gripe is that this really should have been folded into the first Capcom Arcade Stadium instead of split across two separate products — I've lost count of how many times I launched the wrong collection looking for a specific game. The lack of online multiplayer for the fighting and co-op games is a genuine missed opportunity that holds it back from being truly great. Buy it on a deep sale and you'll have a good time; just don't expect a polished, seamless experience.", 'omg this sucks lol', "I'm read... i am read... I am ready to... now I am re... i... i'm... I AM READ.... I AM READY TO PLAY NOW THAT I AM NOT INTERUPTED AG.... I am ready to....

Honestly, interuptions, over and over, and over. When I finally get to play, cool! I like the presentation, and perspectives, and filters.", 'Precision platforming meets a heartfelt story about anxiety and growth. Each jump feels like a personal victory. Beautifully crafteed!', 'Neon-drenched streets pulse with life and danger around every corner. After years of polish, it finally delivers on its promise. Stunning redemption!', 'Razor-sharp gameplay paired with evolving storytelling makes every run feel fresh. Dying has never been this addictive. Pure genius!!!!!!!!!!!!!!', 'Nice1', 'Atmosphere is unmatched. Felt tense, excited, and immersed from the first minute!', 'My new favorite game of all time. Already planning my second playthrough!vbf', 'Hours of entertainment! The replay value is huge with multiple endings to discover.', 'Absolutely addictive gameplay! The graphics are stunning and the story kept me hooked for hours. Highly recommend!', 'Free SonSon = fun fun!', 'This game respects your time and offers a smooth sense of advancement. The mechanics are straightforward, progression feels meaningful, and it’s enjoyable whether you check in briefly or let it run passively. A solid and pleasant experience overall.‍', 'I opened this for nostalgia.
I stayed because arcade games are still mean.

Capcom Arcade 2nd Stadium is a collection of classic games that do not care about your comfort, your time, or your feelings. These games were built to eat your quarters, and they have not forgotten their mission.

Everything is fast, loud, and brutally honest. You will die in seconds, learn nothing, try again, and somehow have fun doing it. The rewind and save features help, but the games still remind you who’s in charge.

If you love retro arcade chaos and testing your reflexes, this collection is great. If you expect modern hand-holding or balance, you are about to get humbled by a game from 1989.

Insert coin. Try again.', "ive pretty only played progear and 1 round of final fight and it's more than worth it", "I missed the arcade era so this is pretty cool if you like immersive emulation
There are cabinet, CRT, and arcade filter options
The cabinet buttons actually move as you play which is a nice touch
Grab the complete pack on a nice sale but this is the better of the two
I'm pretty sure you can unpack the rom files like in the delisted genesis collection", "I have only one game here and it's not my fault"]

name: Deadlink
categories: ['Single-player', 'Steam Achievements', 'Full controller support', 'Steam Trading Cards', 'Steam Cloud', 'Family Sharing']
short_description: Get ready to put a sizable dent in the profits of megacorporations in Deadlink, a guns-blazing cyberpunk FPS with roguelite elements. Your mission is to conduct covert ops while piloting a humanoid combat shell. And no, "covert" doesn’t mean "quiet."
detailed_description_raw:
Deadlink is a cyberpunk FPS with roguelite elements. You're an elite operative recruited to the experimental Deadlink project. Pilot an autonomous combat shell, fighting your way through cramped slums, twisted labs, grimy warehouses, and sleek office buildings on a mission to thwart the schemes of the most powerful corporations in the world. Wield a deadly arsenal, upgrade your skills and tech, devastate destructible environments, and slash corporate profit margins — all at the same time.


You are the Corporate Security Agency’s greatest weapon, and it's your job to make sure the corporations and their shady deals are anything but secure. With the team at HQ backing you up, you're tossed headfirst into the fray, where a military operation with plausible deniability can fix things for a fraction of the cost of a hopeless court case.


Every randomized run will be different, but one thing never changes: you will be outnumbered. Get ready to think on your feet as you dash, jump, and use the environment to your advantage. You can't still, not even for a second.


Equip your shell with powerful weapon loadouts. Try out various combinations as you blaze a path of destruction. Want to snipe your foes from a distance, debilitate them with status effects, or just go in guns blazing? Whatever your playstyle, you can find the right kit for the job.


Your enemies have no shortage of bullets and bodies to throw at you. But you're no slouch either. Sure, you might lose a combat shell here and there, but your next one will be even tougher. Start each new run with amped-up skills and deadlier firepower!

reviews:
['it’s good. it’s a good game. however i have some gripes with it, a lot more than i usually have with games. 23 hours for the 100% so i have some experience. it is a deeply flawed game. lets start out with some positives…

gunplay is pretty incredible, and the whole “mark enemies or die” mechanic is great, it’s fun and gets your noggin joggin. you can kill them, but your kit is on cooldown, so do you risk it and wait? or like finding scenarios of huge marks one ability pop, and grouping enemies for that purpose. it’s really interactive and a big difference from other shooters. guns look amazing, they sound good, they feel good, they hit good. their kit feels relatively balanced, EXCEPT for the fact some augments do basically nothing to engineers gauss rifle, i feel like that’s a bit of an oversight. it still performs well.

the abilities are really nice, all the shells have movement abilities in one way or another. they all feel very unique. but i do feel that there’s shell upgrades that should’ve just… been in the main kit? it’s weird, it feels like i always want them and the class feels incomplete without it. stuff like soldiers gripwire reset on kill upgrade, or hunters c ball translocation upgrade, or juggs cooldown lower on enemies hit with graviton punch, or engys double turret. these all should’ve been base in my opinion, perhaps with a tradeoff. playing them without it feels anywhere from weird to rough. i think they’d feel a lot better to play knowing you don’t have an immediate “need immediately” shell upgrade. the rest feel very balanced and situational. you eventually get hub upgrades that can grant you 3 kits at the start of runs, and you can hope it has it there. or just restart but that’s a bit out of the spirit of it, still, it’s a bit lame to encourage resetting like, these should have been base or a choice in the kit before even playing. it’s not good to discourage building on the fly in games like this, knowing your next shell upgrade is set in stone basically.

the areas… they’re okay. they’re fun. but there’s so little of them that the game feels a bit stale. i was actually very disappointed seeing this, it helps keep games fresh. are the bosses different? no, the only real randomization is the fourth areas mini bosses. i think the fourth area is done really well, and i also love the mini bosses in 1-2, they’re so fun and have such character. the areas look great, it’s just they do run their course. that can be fine, games like noita have this, but in noita it fits the game much much better as you’re striving to reach these areas, you have to actually plan for it. 

the story is not good, it’s very forgettable, the characters are very unlikable, and the voice acting kills it even more. the story just kinda happens with small cutscenes, it’s whatever. it’s there. you don’t play games like this for the story but i find it weird how half assed it is. one character in particular you get her and she never shuts up i cannot stand her. maria or something. would love to disable va as an option. ymmv.

for the other modes, we have essentially time trial challenges (chronodeck), and endless (extant existence). LOVE chronodeck, it’s a ton of fun. endless is just endless, so it’s there, done pretty well although not the best. highly recommend chronodeck it’s a great time.

the problem is that this game is trying to do to way too much. that’s okay by itself. but it doesn’t do any element particularly well, except the gunplay. it’s a bit of a shame, there’s a great framework here, the roguelite elements and upgrades are super cool but just seem not all there. 

i’m gonna recommend this game with a caveat: it’s not gonna be some mind blowing experience. but it is fun if you take it for what it is. and if you like these games, maybe you’re a doom eternal and roguelite fan, you have something decent here. i enjoyed my time with it, yet i still feel a bit sad at what it could’ve been. it is enjoyable but in a way where you’re kinda wishing for more. so if you take all that and roll with it, you’ll have a decently good time. oh yeah, music is good too.

it’s fine, it’s fun, but it is very, VERY flawed. it’s not gonna rock your socks off but it’s an alright game. probably rate it 6/10ish.', 'IMMA PLAGARISE THIS GAME', 'A Hard roguelike with fast pase shooting but fun', "it's okay the biggest problem with the game is that you only have 4 stages and 4 bosses even if you continue to switch between the 4 classes the game gets very old very fast because almost every run plays the same with minor changes you can't change your primary weapon only your secondary and only at set spots so every run feels almost the same", 'This game is just poorly thought out. 

There so many bizarre systems and stupid interactions between them, the cooldown on your main tools just feels to long, but they can't be too short because that's how you activate your buffs (which is a bizarre problem they made for themselves). It feels bad to just not have any upgrades unless you're close enough to blast someone with the melee stun. Also, the dash and doublejump feel awful, they're stubby and conserve no momentum at all, you feel dead in the water after using them. And they're not even that helpful to dash, because dodging gunfire in this game is a complete trainwreck. Like there's these soliders in level 2 that throw these poison mortars at you, but the only real warning is a soft "Pomp!" in the midst of blaring techno and gunfire, and a reticle on the ground. Seems simple, right?

This isn't an MMO. I'm not constantly looking at the ground to see if I'm getting blasted with a DOT effect. Playing like that is suicide, especially with all the floating orbs they want you to pop to get ammo back and move around.

Also, the second boss fight is ludicriously hard because it spawns enemies with absolutely no warning, sometimes you kill one and there's a copy of that spawning robot drone behind you in the air and you have no way of knowing until you take a ♥♥♥♥ ton of damage.

so, yeah. Lots of ideas, none of which work together well. Fun for like 20 minutes and then rapidly becomes frustrating. 

Can I not just shoot someone? Do I have to play a warioware minigame so I can get my buffs first?', "This game is not bad but some of the mechaniques make 0 sense to me from a gameplay aspect the orbs floating around almost take from the gameplay rather than add something fun it like playing doom eternal but you have to remeber to punch random floating orbs to do a simple buff like 10% bonus damage or increase your fire rate. It actually breaks the flow of the game i would rather it wasn't there at all and grenade do neglagible dmg compared to your main weapon or even the melee, its aoe is tiny i think i've hit at most 2 enemies at onece with grenades.
The aestatic is pretty uninspired idk how this got above a 7/10 2023 must of been a slow year. Enemies are fun to fight i wouldn't spend more than 10$ on this game tbh. Their are better indie fps and doom shooters. Def play the demo before pulling out your wallet for this one.", 'It is fun in short instances. You probably will not spend hours in one session for multiple runs, but for a short session of one or two runs the game is a fun roguelite shooter. There are 4 playable classes with different weapons and abilities that will keep things fresh and variable.', "It was ok.

I stopped after beating it on easy, nothing made me come back for more.

I felt particularly sweet and sorrow regarding the balance of it being an actual shooter and random effects and explosions. As in you can't shoot you're way through a level, your gameplay has to be tied to grenades/abilities to get your shield back, I wasn't really fond of that.", "The game is a bit over-designed in my opinion and interacts too much with non-FPS mechanics, making combat feel distracting rather than focused. Too many enemies require the use of non-FPS mechanics, such as the stunning ability, to meaningfully interact with, and I just don't like constantly having to do that. If such enemies are rare it would be more bearable but in order to get even the basic amount of healing/shield you recharge you have to keep marking enemies and that requires using grenades or abilities, so it's just constantly forcing me to do non-FPS things and it's just very distracting and annoying.

I would much prefer the game if marking merely increases the amount of recharge you get and make it easier to deal with certain enemies, rather than being so fundamentally required in moment-to-moment gameplay.", 'if doom eternal was a rougelike i.e. resource management sim', 'Fun game.', 'this game ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ slaps. The combat feels fluent and easy to get into, while being difficult enough to where you need to have at the very least a basic understanding of how the game works to do well. It also allows you to feel badass in a similar way to ultrakill, in that instead of showing you a cool flashy cutscene like a glory kill to make you feel cool, it forces you to actually get decent and engage with the mechanics, leading to you feeling badass for something you actually did and actually had to work for. The shells were also masterfully done. Each shell brings different things to the table, while also not straying too far from each other. beautifully crafted game', "What if Roboquest was Cyberpunk and it was freaking awesome

Edit (after 100%): 100%ed the game now and honestly was really impressed. I really REALLY love action fps roguelikes/lites and this is one of the better ones I've played, up there with Roboquest for me. I really like how the gadgets and shell upgrades work and I find it a very unique experience in terms of how the player gets more powerful. Although some gadgets and upgrades are very basic, as a whole, they help make the game feel fluid, and give a lot of weight to ability usage, as well as giving a lot of options for build variety and playstyle expression. Same thing with the weapon upgrades, for the most part they are genuinely really interesting and can completely change how the weapons feel and I really love it. 

Before I ramble about what I dislike, I really like this game and if you're a roguelike enthusiast like myself definitely give this one ago. However if you're like me and want to get 200+ fps on every game I'd be weary because this game kinda runs like hot ass for what it is. Apart from that solid 8/10 game

My only real criticisms for the game revolve around some of the enemy designs and with the general scaling. I will confidently say I am generally very good at games such as this, I certainly have a lot of transferable skills from other roguelikes such as Roboquest that have similar gameplay. And although I found the harder levels of Roboquest a blast, I found that this games hardest difficulty just feels like ♥♥♥♥. I get that healing overall should never be a crutch and it you should always feel punished for being hit. But difficulty 3 in this game is just an over-scaled ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, I'm yet to do a run on diff 3 where it actually feels like I am rewarded for my builds and mechanical ability, but rather I just hit a jackpot with gadgets and upgrades that are a tad over-tuned (particularly money shot and basically every shell upgrade that refreshes a mark ability). It could be a skill issue, but I honestly don't know if I am going to want to get all the weapon skins and ♥♥♥♥, due to me not really enjoying diff 3.", 'too noisy, so many colors, sounds', "although this game can be extremely fun, it's just so unstable. in just a little over an hour of playing i've dealt with 3-4 crashes and the game refusing to start, forcing me to disable some windows security setting just to run it. if it ever gets fixed or just becomes more stable then it'd be an easy recommendation from me, but as it stands it's very much not.", 'Deadlink is a solid roguelike experience with a fast-paced cyberpunk vibe. Some of the levels are genuinely enjoyable and well-designed, and the gameplay loop keeps things engaging. I mainly played it for the achievements, but it was still a fun ride overall.

❤️', 'فنانه

', "Excellent little condensed roguelite FPS game with an EXTREMELY addicting power fantasy gameplay loop that especially excels in its movement mechanics. Whilst this game isn't quite as content packed as other games in the genre I firmly believe that what is accomplished here is accomplished very well and it's packaged with one of the best OST's I've ever heard in a game before. For the price, it's hard to go wrong with this one, though just taper your expectations a bit as this is the kind of game you'll likely see all there is to see of within 30-40 hours which is a little light for a roguelite but the time you spend with the game will be quite top notch. His turret is ok I guess, but you HAVE to rely on it for any sort of hope of succeeding.

Overall though, pretty fun. Catch it on sale for a fun fps challenge.', 'its pretty trash honestly.', 'Deadlink, developed by Gruby Entertainment and published by SuperGG.com, is a high-velocity cyberpunk first-person shooter that fuses classic arena-style gunplay with modern roguelite structure. From the outset, the game establishes its identity as an unapologetically aggressive, movement-driven experience. You step into the role of an elite operative remotely controlling a combat shell, sent on high-risk corporate sabotage missions in a dystopian future where megacorporations dominate everything. The premise is straightforward and deliberately functional, serving primarily as a framework to justify the relentless forward momentum of each run rather than anchoring the experience in dense narrative exposition.

What truly defines Deadlink is its kinetic combat loop. Movement is fast, fluid, and essential to survival. The design philosophy clearly draws inspiration from old-school shooters, where standing still is a death sentence and spatial awareness is as important as firepower. Enemies flood arenas in waves, forcing you to dash, slide, and reposition constantly while juggling cooldowns and weapon swaps. Gunplay feels punchy and responsive, with a strong emphasis on precision and rhythm. Weapons range from rapid-fire assault rifles and high-impact shotguns to more exotic futuristic armaments, each offering distinct tactical advantages. The feedback loop of landing shots, triggering abilities, and weaving through incoming fire creates a satisfying flow state that keeps adrenaline high throughout each encounter.

Layered over this traditional shooter foundation is a roguelite progression system that shapes how each run unfolds. Levels are procedurally arranged, and between combat arenas you select upgrades, perks, and modifiers that alter your build. These can dramatically shift your playstyle, encouraging experimentation. One run might emphasize raw weapon damage and aggressive close-quarters dominance, while another leans into status effects, drone support, or ability cooldown reduction. Permanent unlocks outside of runs add a sense of long-term progression, rewarding persistence even after failed attempts. This structure gives Deadlink significant replay value, as no two sessions play out exactly the same, and mastering different build paths becomes part of the challenge.

Visually, the game embraces a stylized cyberpunk aesthetic saturated in neon light, industrial grit, and metallic architecture. Corporate skyscrapers, underground labs, and high-tech industrial complexes create environments that feel cold and hostile, reinforcing the dystopian tone. While it does not attempt hyperrealism, the art direction leans into bold contrasts and clean silhouettes that ensure clarity during chaotic firefights. Particle effects, explosions, and weapon animations amplify the sense of impact without overwhelming visibility. The soundtrack complements the pacing with driving electronic beats that intensify combat encounters, maintaining tension and momentum across each stage.

Boss encounters serve as climactic tests of both mechanical skill and build efficiency. These fights are designed around pattern recognition, spatial control, and quick adaptation. Unlike standard enemies, bosses often introduce mechanics that punish complacency, requiring players to read attack telegraphs carefully while maintaining offensive pressure. These battles highlight the strengths of the game’s movement system and often determine whether a build truly synergizes or collapses under pressure.

Despite its strengths, Deadlink is not without limitations. Over extended play sessions, the procedural environments can begin to feel structurally familiar, even if enemy placement and upgrades vary. Certain upgrade combinations can overshadow others in terms of efficiency, potentially narrowing optimal build diversity at higher difficulties. The narrative, while thematically aligned with cyberpunk tropes of corporate corruption and technological overreach, remains relatively understated and primarily serves as atmospheric context rather than a driving force. Players seeking a story-heavy campaign may find the emphasis firmly placed on mechanics over plot.

Where Deadlink ultimately succeeds is in delivering a focused, polished gameplay experience that understands exactly what it wants to be. It does not aim to revolutionize the FPS genre or redefine roguelite conventions; instead, it refines and merges them into a cohesive, high-intensity package. The constant tension between risk and reward, the satisfaction of mastering movement and aim, and the thrill of assembling a powerful build all contribute to an experience that feels skill-based yet rewarding over time. For fans of fast shooters who enjoy iterative progression and mechanical mastery, Deadlink offers a challenging and energetic ride that stands confidently among modern indie action titles.

name: Inside the Backrooms
categories: ['Single-player', 'Multi-player', 'Co-op', 'Online Co-op', 'Steam Achievements', 'Tracked Controller Support', 'Steam Workshop', 'Family Sharing']
short_description: Inside the Backrooms is a horror multiplayer game mixed with different mechanics that will make you spend an intense night with friends. Explore, think, interact, but be careful, it is rumored that there are several entities wishing to find you...
detailed_description_raw:
DESCRIPTION

Inside the Backrooms is a Co-op online horror game up to 4 players, where you and your friends will fight to escape from the different levels of the backrooms, solving different puzzles with different mechanics in each one.

This game is based on the famous creepypasta with many real references implemented such as iconic entities and important elements. You will have to explore each room, look for elements that help you to continue advancing throughout the game and unlock new areas, but the further you go, more dangerous it will be, you must pay close attention in the area, identify each entity and know how to avoid them if you want to survive.

Look for supplies, store them in your inventory, explore all the rooms, solve puzzles, unlock areas of the map, interact, try to collect everything you find.

The main objective of Inside the Backrooms is to engage players with its gameplay, difficulty and atmosphere. There are countless identical rooms with an old, dirty carpet and pale yellowish walls, where at first glance it gives you a gloomy and bad feeling where you realize that it is not a safe place.

LEVELS

The game currently has 5 levels, each level is huge and is designed to be more difficult than the previous one and each one with different puzzles and mechanics.

There are also 2 secret mini levels that will lead you to a secret ending..

ENTITIES

In this game you will find several entities, currently 6 entities, all based on the original creepypasta, each entity will have different ways of attacking and manifesting, so it will be important to know it's behavior of each of these and avoid at all costs that several entities finds you at the same time, if this happens, you will have a bad time.

PUZZLES

Inside the Backrooms has a lot of diverse, interesting and entertaining puzzles, from simple ones to the complex and laborious, they are carefully elaborated with the purpouse of being solved both individually and cooperatively, in addition to this, to make the puzzles more dynamic and attractive, in each new game the solution of all of them changes, which means that it wont be always the same answer! so test your puzzle resolution skills and try to escape from this terrifying place full of mysteries.

ITEMS

If you want to survive in the backrooms you will need to look for essential items and manage them properly, because if you get short of any of them, it could mean your death.

These are some of the items you can find on your journey through the backrooms:

  • Almond Water: Regenerate part of life.

  • Pills: Eliminate Anxiety

  • Radios: Allows communication at a distance with your team

  • Flashlight: Allows you to see in dark areas.

  • Protection Suit: Allows you to prevent getting radiation on some areas.

  • Documents and Notes: Provides relevant information.

FEATURES

Here are some of the current features of the game:

  • Proximity Voice Chat: Talk to the nearest people using the voice chat, but be careful, some entities could hear you...

  • Stamina: The player will have limited stamina that you will need to manage correctly, if an entity is close to you, it will be your best ally to escape.

  • Inventory: You will be able to take various items and store them in your inventory, however, space is limited.

  • Anxiety: When you see an entity, your state of anxiety will increase, going through a disorder of fear and poor visibility.

  • Entities: Each entity has its own attack modes, learn their techniques to evade them.

  • Respawn: The game incorpore a creative and dynamic way to respawn, you will able to respawn up to 3 times.

DEVELOPMENT ROADMAP

We want to keep improving the game, to the point where it becomes one of the best games that represent backroom creepypasta. For this reason we will continue to polishing and adding new content to the game, these are some features that are planned to be added throughout the final development of the game:

  • New Entities

  • More Levels

  • New Puzzles and Mechanics

  • Procedural Levels

  • New Items

  • Improve Gameplay

  • Language Localization

Don't be like me and spend money on this garbage.", "AI slop, what more do we need? Lazy gameplay, lazy puzzles and literally nothing a roblox game hasn't done before. It also looks horrible.", '*kid sitting on pepsi* your ai slop bores me', 'Realized it had AI art', 'Where do I even start.

I last played this game in September of 2022 and played it all the way through (the content it had at that time), I didn't hate it but I definitely could see the flaws in it's design. I joined in with a group of friends in 2026 and what I played was an absolute cluster ♥♥♥♥ of an experience, enough to warrant this review.

Scuffed Audio.
Bad Entity Design.
Atrocious Level Design and Horrid Puzzle Design.

I don't want to bash the dev too hard as you can very clearly tell this is one of the first few games they've ever made, even shipping a game as a indie dev deserves the upmost respect and as an aspiring game dev myself I do understand how hard it is to get to that point. Just keep this in mind as I don't want to discourage this dev from continuing development or stop them from continuing to learn and grow as a dev. With that being said I will share my honest experience.

The first thing I encountered was absolutely scuffed audio, what do I mean by this? Buggy and inaccurate audio, first, it's buggy because it crackles and sometimes an entire side of one of the channels (L/R) turns off or is silent enough to sound like it's off. Second is the inaccuracy, either the monsters or when people talk sound like they are right next to you even though they are 50-100 feet away from you, this also makes it hard to distinguish which of your friends are who in physical space because everyone sounds like they are right next to you or coming from the same general area, this happens regardless of audio settings. The bugginess of the audio seems like just a screw up during development and might be an easy fix if it's something on the engine's end, the inaccuracy of the audio might be harder to fix.

The entity design. The AI can easily be "baited" into an attack which misses, over and over. It just seems like something that should not be possible. Even then the entities seemed to be designed around smashing your head against the wall, you aren't intended to outwit them, outrun them or in general survive them, this is even more so confirmed by the fact each level gives you 10 lives, where you are able to respawn your character up to 10 times, it shows that dying up to 10 times is the intended experience. The entities could have balanced around the player instead it feels as if the player was given more lives in place of that balance. Item usage is also hampered by the entity design, items take a while to use and usually require opening your inventory and then an additional 2 clicks to use if not bound to your limited 3 allotted hotkeys, this encourages a more preemptive use of the items whereas the entities are designed around a more impulsive use of the items, as it's hard to predict when an entity will agro to you as they either agro from off-screen or the audio is so inaccurate that you don't hear them or even misjudge where they are physically from you, they are so absurdly fast that any use of an item is likely to kill you if it doesn't heal you. The sanity mechanic is so unbelievably over the top as well, if you don't have pills to counter it you are basically dead if an entity agros, not to mention how disorienting it is normally, it needs to be rebalanced or the effect needs to be redesigned or replaced.

The level design. The backrooms are disorienting by nature and you are meant to feel lost in them, but that is the issue, you are meant to FEEL lost in them, not actually get lost in them, especially for a level based game as this is, you want to get to the next level, you want to feel that sense of progression. The issue here is not that the backrooms feel like a maze but that you know exactly where you need and want to go, but you have to find the singular entrance in the maze that's for that specific section of the level, basically you have to find a needle in a haystack whenever you want to go to the different sections of the level, this doesn't feel fun whatsoever. The feedback I'm wanting to give is to open up the backrooms levels a bit more, eliminate those single entrances to different sections of the backrooms, it would feel so much better to play.

The puzzle design. The puzzle's concepts themselves are okay, it's the execution of them that are flawed, almost all the puzzles in this game are memory puzzles, information you find and need to remember for an extended period of time, of course you can write stuff down, take screenshots or pictures with your phone, maybe write in a group chat. I wouldn't normally have an issue with a good ol memory puzzle, the problem comes with how you find out this information, you will often find clues or solutions or keys to puzzle elements in very obscure corners of this maze, and you will often find clues to puzzles you aren't even close to yet and still you are expected to remember this information, or god forbid trek all the way back through this maze to re-read these pieces of information. I'm not sure if the dev is a fan of memory puzzles or what but it would explain it. What I mean to say is the puzzles do not feel natural, they don't feel like "oh duh of course that's what needs to be done!" they often just leave you more confused than if they were just left unsolved. I say these things as someone who likes puzzle games and is currently going through a puzzle game binge.

I'd say these are the order in which I think these should be addressed:
1. Audio Issues (As it will probably be relatively simple)
2. Levels
3. Puzzles
4. Entities

I hope this game will improve and I believe it can with the proper effort, I will keep an eye on further development and update my review if anything changes. Thank you for reading and if the dev is reading, keep your chin up!', 'Bad', "MONSTERS SHUDN'T STUNLOCK YOU

store_description: About This Game The world is a vast, beautiful, and dangerous place – especially when you have been shrunk to the size of an ant. Explore, build and survive together in this first-person, multiplayer, survival adventure. Can you thrive alongside the hordes of giant insects, fighting to survive the perils of the backyard? Uncover the mysteries while playing through the story! How did you wind up so small? Who did this to you? How do you go home? These are all answers you will uncover as you play through the story. Solo or with friends – anytime! You can face the backyard alone or together, online, with up to three friends. Not only that, but with the Shared Worlds feature, you can continue to play in your shared world even if the original host is not on, with all your progression saving! Nowhere is safe – not even your base! Creatures can be found roaming the yard in a multitude of environments, such as the depths of the pond, the caverns of the termite den, and even the sweltering heat found in the sandbox. You can even attract them to different places in the yard by activating the MIX.R devices. However, the more you interfere with the creatures in the yard, the higher the chance that they come knocking at your own door, so you better prepare. Play true to your playstyle! Use the in-game customization systems such as Mutations and Milk Molars to activate the bonuses and perks you want for your character. Not only that, but craft and upgrade your armor and weapons to give your character the stats and advantages you need in order to take on the perils of the backyard. It’s time to go big, or never go home!

detailed_description_raw:
Buzz


About the Game




Sherlock Holmes: The Devil’s Daughter is a fantastic adventure with unique gameplay that blends investigation, action and exploration for an extraordinary experience that will test the limits of your nerves and intelligence.

Track down evil in the darkest corners of London and the human soul while playing as the great detective, as you untangle a web of intrigue leading to the final stunning revelation.

Each of your deductions and actions affects the rest of the story, for better or for worse…

  • Play as Sherlock Holmes and use his extraordinary abilities to progress through the adventure.

store_description: About This Game Sherlock Holmes: The Devil’s Daughter is a fantastic adventure with unique gameplay that blends investigation, action and exploration for an extraordinary experience that will test the limits of your nerves and intelligence. Track down evil in the darkest corners of London and the human soul while playing as the great detective, as you untangle a web of intrigue leading to the final stunning revelation. Each of your deductions and actions affects the rest of the story , for better or for worse… Play as Sherlock Holmes and use his extraordinary abilities to progress through the adventure. Freely explore several of the city's neighbourhoods in search of clues and suspects. Interrogations, combat, chases, infiltration… discover a game that is unlike any other!
reviews:
['Why is Sherlock sexy in this?', '[h3]This is not a detective game. This is torture.[/h3]

I have closed one case, out of many, I assume, and I have had enough. Every detail feels wrong. Let me explain what this game did wrong in two words:

[b]ABUSIVE HANDHOLDING[/b]

You know, a true detective game, or maybe just my weird expectation of one, is a game where you actually do detective work. You investigate things, draw conclusions, and your knowledge moves the story forward. Knowledge.

Here, the game moves you forward, explains every little step you have to take, and if you want any freedom of choice, exploration, or investigation, you will find nothing.

I found a note about a missing person, and it even had an address! Of course I want to go there, it is literally on the next street. But no, there is a padlock in the way, and you cannot get to house number 5 because the street somehow ends at 9. Your clues are not yours. You have to do what the game tells you to do. Or suffer.

You know what I really have to do? Activate Sherlock's spider-sense and pixel-hunt around a location I already spent the last 20 minutes walking through without any luck. I do not remember signing up for a hidden object game.

That is why I call it abusive. The game not only forces you to follow its strict plot, it also mocks your detective skills by putting you through tedious exploration. Not careful note-reading, not actual deduction, just clicking on everything and hoping Sherlock finally says out loud what you already figured out.

The deduction system is still completely wrong. You click through a bunch of tags like a character from Idiocracy. Connecting the dots only counts when the game wants it to, not when it happens naturally because you actually followed the story and figured things out on your own. You have to build this whole rigid hierarchy as if only two things can be related at a time, otherwise the full picture cannot be restored.

You know what game does [b]DEDUCTION[/b] correctly? [b]DUCK DETECTIVE![/b]
That game was funny, clumsy, and childish, but you still had to reach conclusions based on your [b]own[/b] analysis. Here, you just turn off your brain and play mini-games.

OH YES, MINI-GAMES!

I hate them with a pure passion. This is exactly how not to do mini-games in a detective game. They are either annoying or just stupid and boring. Looking through 5+ keyholes, clicking on garbage, and listening to Sherlock say "just garbage" five times in a row. What a meta-commentary. I had the exact same thought while playing this game.

Then the game suddenly tries to become some kind of GTA-style chase sequence, or The Witcher 3 with magical detective vision guiding you through swamps. And the most absurd thing of all is [b]PLAYING AS A DOG[/b]. Hilarious. Sherlock stays behind, but I have one question: how does the dog manage to open doors?! It has paws! It cannot open doors like that!

I do not understand the tone at all. Sometimes the game tries to be overly realistic. Fine, children, let us take a blood sample and mix it with three different liquids. For each liquid, you have to click three times. Rinse and repeat. And then suddenly the DOG MAGICALLY OPENS DOORS.

[i]"Detective" as a genre was butchered.
"Adventure" will make you die of boredom.
"Mystery" will not be yours to solve.
"Puzzle" means hunting for pixels.
"Story Rich" is nowhere near enough to justify everything else.[/i]', "First playthrough review time.

Sherlock Holmes: The Devil's Daughter is a mystery game where you play as Sherlock Holmes. The game takes place in London, and you need to solve five different cases. You will explore places, find clues, and talk to people to solve the mysteries. 

The game looks nice, with good character designs and detailed places. The London setting feels dark, with fog and street lamps. The voices and movements of the characters are well done, which makes the game feel real. The story has many surprises, so it may keep players interested.

I bought Sherlock Holmes: The Devil's Daughter on Steam in May 2021 for 1 dollar, which was the lowest price I’d ever seen on a Steam sale. I didn’t start playing it until this year because I wanted to finish the earlier Sherlock Holmes games first before moving on to this one.

In the end, this is a game for people who like solving mysteries. It has a good mix of exploring, finding clues, and some action. While some people might not like the action parts, the story and graphics make the game captivating for anyone who likes mysteries.", "It's a mixed game it's good but you feel like the devs were not focused while making it or something
but overall 8/10
you can get happy with it especially if it's your first sherlock holmes game to play.", "Some cases do not make sense as for who the real killer is. But it is a very interesting story and I loved Sherlock's relationship with his daughter. <3", "After playing The Testament of Sherlock Holmes and Crimes & Punishments, I finally had the chance to play and finish this game in about a day. Overall it was fun, but had a lot of ups and downs.

First of all, the loading times were atrocious. Considering that sometimes you have to go from x to y to back to x and then z, it might get frustrating for some people. Graphics were much better than the previous games but there were some inconsistencies such as Sherlock's and Watson's faces being very detailed whereas Alice's and Kate's faces looked like the textures were constantly trying to load. Animations were inconsistent too, sometimes they looked good and natural and sometimes they were just clunky and lacking, like when you slap [spoiler] Alice [/spoiler], Sherlock just stands there and suddenly you see the slap animation end.

Gameplay wise, I enjoy QTEs in games and this game had A LOT of them which I personally didn't mind. However, you should know that some QTEs have very short time limits that could make it a bit frustrating for controllers. Like in the previous games, you have a deduction board in which you can connect different evidences to bring up a deduction. There are absolute deductions which act both as an information and evidence for variable deductions. Variable deductions on the other hand, present you two deductions to choose from and depending on your answer it will direct you to a possible culprit. The thing that most bothered me was, in previous games collecting evidence and using this board was much more easier and logical but this game never really presents you a concrete evidence. Even connecting different evidences don't give you a better idea on what is going on. You have to REALLY nitpick some documents and info the game presents you as having little importance because if not, the game will tell you the culprit was Y with the most *sspull move [spoiler] looking at you 'pygmy' [/spoiler] when EVERYTHING was directing you to X. Also for some reason Sherlock was dumber? He didn't have much to say nor think. And not being able to use the analysis table as much as previous games was a huge let down for me. Same goes for autopsies and much detailed place searches. I think this superficial gameplay was the reason deductions didn't work as good as it should've been.

The actual story was short and maybe kinda rushed as we could only really focus on it at the end. While I know the point is that Sherlock wasn't attentive at all, I would love to see a better relationship between Sherlock and Kate. It looks like Sherlock should've had a character development with Kate, going from a cocky detective to still cocky but somewhat passionate and kind dad, but instead of going with either of them they just watered down all his traits. [spoiler] Only at the end he finally realizes he wasn't being a good dad, and you don't even know what happens after. [/spoiler] The storytelling felt like someone edited a movie and cut the most important plot parts from it. The case stories were interesting and while still confusing at times, much more consistent than the main story.

Sherlock Holmes: The Devil's Daughter was a mess compared to previous games but it was still enjoyable. Don't be hesitant to use the Skip option in puzzles or look for help online in your deductions if needed as they might be too frustrating or dragging you for too long.", 'When Investigative Genius Collides with Poor Narrative Design (Spoiler Alert)



As a reader and fan of Arthur Conan Doyle's novels, I began Sherlock Holmes: The Devil's Daughter with expectations based on the principles of deductive reasoning. The game delivers a wildly inconsistent experience; at times, it gives you a real intellectual thrill with clever deductive reasoning, and at others, it frustrates you with illogical design choices and blatant narrative inconsistencies. Here's a critical breakdown of my experience with the game's cases:

1. Abandoning Logic in Favor of Stereotypes (Case Two)
The "Study in Green" case is a glaring example of prioritizing superficial excitement over logic. During the investigation, the logical evidence clearly pointed to "Marley". The conclusion that he used reverse engineering and a mechanical statue full of gears to throw a spear is a clever solution perfectly suited to the Industrial Revolution era and the genius of the Holmes universe.

But the game forces an "official" solution that completely defies physics: a man with an amputated arm and his dwarf partner manage to move a massive metal statue, and the dwarf throws a metal spear that pierces a grown man's body from a distance! Relying on flimsy justifications like the "Mayan curse" cliché makes the official solution feel lazy and disappointing.

2. Wasting the Best Plot and Breaking the "Chekhov's Gun" Principle (Case Three)
In storytelling and dramatic writing, the "Chekhov's Gun" principle simply states: "If you describe a gun hanging on the wall in Act One, it must be fired in Act Two or Three, otherwise it has no purpose."

The game breaks this principle in the "Infamy" case with the character Orson Wilde in a blatantly frustrating manner. The script piled Chekhov's guns around him throughout the investigation, but none of them were fired in the end:

The Mystery of the Gaze (Jeremiah's Death): The cinematic focus on the gaze exchanged between Orson and Jeremiah after Orson stabbed him was not arbitrary; it was portrayed as the climax. In any literary work, this scene would be the "hanging gun." If Percy (the gang leader) was the actual mastermind behind the bomb attack, as the game claims, then this gaze loses all dramatic significance, transforming from a key to solving the mystery into an empty, unexplained shot.

The Burning Paper: The scene of Orson boldly snatching the paper from Holmes's hand and quickly burning it in front of him is a major event that would angry any detective. What was its content? And why did he burn it in that way? The game completely ignores this event and leaves it unresolved.

The Psychological Depth: The tampering with Holmes's belongings and the intrusive interference in his cases (such as the Mary Sutherland case) were portrayed as part of a dangerous, pathological obsession with impersonating Holmes and "stealing his life." This brilliant psychological approach is abruptly abandoned in favor of framing a traditional gang, turning Percy Fleming, the professional crime boss, into a fool who risks his empire and attracts the attention of all of London's police forces for a mere personal vendetta!

3. The Trap of Physical Evidence and the Inconsistency in Character Writing (Case Four)
In the "Chain Reaction" case, subtle psychological evidence is absent, and the crime scene becomes the primary evidence. The deduction space was one of the most enjoyable mechanics.

The game successfully employed effective red herrings, such as the anarchist Thomas, who would seem the easiest suspect were it not for the fact that anarchists seek public destruction and don't secretly kill their partners in the sewers.

However, the deeper problem lies with the true culprit, the electrician Reginald Butcher. The game falls into a glaring narrative contradiction that makes his exclusion logically justifiable for any player relying on careful deduction. The game asks you to believe that he is the mastermind who engineered a complex electrical trap and robbed an underground drain, while simultaneously showing him meticulously cleaning his shoes amidst all the chaos, reflecting a disciplined and calm approach to covering his tracks. However, in a frustrating contradiction, the game presents him as someone who suspiciously neglects his job duties, rather than being meticulously careful and diligent to avoid suspicion. Furthermore, his brazen and reckless escape from prison is entirely inconsistent with the extreme rationality and planning required to commit such a crime. Even when the player applies the principle of "evidence by absence," they will notice the complete absence of any children's belongings in the room where the electric shock occurred. While this could be attributed to the daughter receiving treatment elsewhere, the accumulation of these behavioral and material inconsistencies makes the game seem to lack a clear understanding of this character. Is he a calm and disciplined genius? Or a bewildered father acting with blind desperation and stupidity? This inconsistency represents a fatal flaw in the case's design and logical coherence.

4. Betrayal of the Original and the Illusion of Choice (Conclusion)
The fifth and final case, Fever Dreams, is the ultimate blunder. As a reader of the Sherlock Holmes novels, Holmes's family is insignificant in his world and character background (with the exception of Mycroft). Stripping Sherlock of his usual cool logic and thrusting him into a contrived family drama with an adopted daughter is a frustrating departure from the essence of the character and completely alien to the world of investigation.

The real downfall occurs in the final dialogue with Alice. Throughout the game, you are forced to make moral decisions (forgive or condemn), only to discover that Alice's words are merely a recap of your choices with no real impact on saving Kate or on the game's ending. The game has sold players the "illusion of choice" to conclude with a forced, linear path.

Conclusion
Sherlock Holmes: The Devil's Daughter offers excellent moments in terms of crime scene analysis and connecting physical evidence, but it falters badly in narrative coherence. The game ignores dramatic writing principles, abandons deep psychological plots in favor of cliché and clumsy solutions, and offers a meaningless, illusory decision-making system. It is an experience that may satisfy those seeking an atmosphere of visual mystery, but it will certainly provoke investigators who are looking for well-crafted criminal plots that do not underestimate their intelligence or ignore their own evidence.


', "Splendid!

Want to mention the excellent voice acting — for example, in the gangster bars, the characters speak the same accent that Peaky Blinders fans recognize from the first second.

Exciting Sherlock cases in which the player decides the fate of the guilty, as well as identifies them — it doesn't always seem obvious, and sometimes it's extremely unexpected.

I also can't help but mention the wonderful and sassy Dr. Watson (you can even play as him)!

The only thing I didn't particularly like was the way the story of Holmes' daughter was handled. In the familiar world of Sherlock that the game presents, it feels somewhat abrupt and out of place, like a small throw-in.

Otherwise, I highly recommend this game to fans of detective stories, especially Sherlock Holmes.", 'Pretty awesome!', 'Gripping game, liked the cases but some of the minigames killed the vibe.
Has high potential replayability!', "Sherlock Holmes: The Devil's Daughter
.
