reviews:
["Visually stunning game. Although it caught me lacking on the story,  it still provided answers to some questions from the last 2 games and provided a conclusion to the Survivor Trilogy. It leaned more into Lara's character development, and Jonah - big W for mah boy.

A solid game overall. Siram sana!", 'Проходил во второй раз, игра крайне пиздатая, единственное часть с попаданием в пайтити и занкомством с "Умурату" слабовата.', 'GAME SUCKS, TRYING TO DO PUZZLES AND NOTHING MOVES WORKS.... I JUST THERE FOR HOURS AND NOTHING HAPPENS. GAME IS POORLY MADE.', "Glitchy af, playing on the hardest setting so I can only save at base camps and it really sucks when she decides to walk off a ledge when you aren't even touching the controller. Also, I move to go up and she decides to go to the side, I try to get her to jump and hook into a climbable spot and she'll glitch out and not do it or get stuck and the only way to get her unstuck is to just make her jump into the void, won't catch ledges and hand holds you direct her to and I've checked to make sure I'm attempting the right spot and I am. It would be fine on any other setting, but it's infuriating when its on the most difficult setting and it sets you back 30-45 minutes multiple times throughout the game. I've got 26 hours into this game because of having to replay 30-45 minutes worth of an area between 5-10 times because of this. I honestly don't think I'm even going to finish it at this point because I'm sick of replaying annoying spots over and over and then hearing her same dialogue 10 times in the same spot I have to redo because the MC decides to have a mind of her own and walk off a ledge or not take controller commands. Not a fan of the super long cut scenes either, to be honest they should've made a movie if they wanted it to have these long cut scenes and all this drama. I wanted a pretty linear survival/combat game with some exploration, not an interactive movie with glitched out mechanics.", '不错，但是劳拉表情没有前两作生动了', "Hard to believe I've played for 65hrs.", 'I played and really enjoyed the previous two Tomb Raider games. Same experience with this one.', 'very good gave with interesting story :) i hope they will release more games like this', 'This was a very worthy conclusion to a trilogy I enjoyed thoroughly. I love how every part adds some new gameplay mechanics while staying true to the general gameplay of its predecessor. I also very much appreciated that this was the only part of the trilogy with HDR, which looked gorgeous in the setting of the south american rain forests. The optional setting to have every character speak their native language is great for learning Spanish, although it was a bit immersion breaking that Lara herself would always respond in English.', 'nice af ♥♥♥♥', "If u haven't played Tomb Raider yet... what are u doing with your life?", '', "I just want to THANK the devs and Crystal Dynamics for how BEAUTIFUL and optimized this game is. The work and art out into this game puts others to shame. I just beat ROTTR and I'm 7% into SOTTR and this game is absolutely stunning. Can't wait for the new ones to come out I'll be ready!", 'amazing game', "Shadow of the Tomb Raider is the end of the trilogy and it’s honestly just more of the same.
The gameplay is still peak. The stealth is the best part—stalking guys in the jungle like a mud-covered predator is badass. The tombs are also the best they’ve ever been; they’re actually complex and fun to solve. Plus, the game looks incredible.
But the story is a total mess. It’s a melodramatic apocalypse plot that’s hard to care about, and the pacing is dragged down by way too many forced walking sections. It doesn't really do anything new, and by the end, it feels like the series was running out of steam.
If you liked the first two, it’s worth a play for the mechanics alone. I’m giving it a 65% on the Moist Meter. It’s a solid game, just not a revolutionary one.", 'They simplified a good game. The cutscenes are too long, the drama is too much. A bad atempt to make the game as the Last of us. Constant cutscenes & monologs. This could have been a great adventure if they actually let you to play it instead of giving you pointers in cutscenes, etc. Alos, there is a few glitches ingame that make you lose your mind and without a restart its impossible to pass some important puzzles. AAA gaming? Gaming or movie making? What do they want? A prize for gaming or a prize for Animation? Isnt this a GAME?', 'awesome, just awesome :)', 'My all time fave : Lara Croft!!!', 'abslolute peak', 'amazing looking game with great performance and a decent story!', '1. Consuming too much memory.
2. Random crashes everytime.
3. World not loading.
4. Audio skipping in some character dialogues. 
5. More bugs', 'Shadow of the Tomb Raider delivers a darker, more introspective Lara Croft, with a strong focus on exploration and tomb puzzles rather than constant combat. The environments are stunning and immersive, especially the dense jungles and hidden ruins. Puzzle design is clever and rewarding, though the story pacing can feel uneven at times. Combat takes a slight backseat, which may not appeal to everyone, but stealth options are satisfying. Overall, it’s a visually rich and thoughtful finale to the reboot trilogy. I enjoyed a lot since its about exploration and adventure.', "Shadow of the Tomb Raider (2018)

Finally got around to finish the Tomb Raider 2013 reboot series. I played the last game way too late and I didn't bother to go back and replay the previous games, so I don't remember much story wise. I remember the bad guys from the previous game and that was enough knowledge to work with. This game's story was wild. But then again, if you played the older Tomb Raider games it's not really surprising.

What can I really say here? It plays like the previous two games, you can still craft arrows on the run, you collect guns to shoot people with and you can do stealth take-downs. If you enjoyed the previous games this one is just as good as those to get. The setting was very cool, you get to run around in the Peruvian jungle as you explore lost tombs. Same mechanics as the previous games from looting salvage to upgrade your gear to ripping out small trees to craft arrows. You still use the pick-axe to climb to higher places and you still do that weird slightly boosting yourself up the wall jump to get to a higher ledge. I suppose my only complaint is the game's sound seems to be broken. When you start up the game there is no dialog, but there are plenty of fixes online to fix it so it was just more annoying than anything really. Also the villians ending was a bit lackluster for me, expected more.

This one is easy - If you played the previous games I see no reason not to complete the trilogy. Start with the 2013 one and see how it plays for you. It's a solid game with a crazy plot, good stuff.

8/10

“Don’t you know you caused all of this Lara!, not everything is about you!”
- Jonah Maiava", 'I dont think they made a single good decision when making this game. half baked, overbloated, corprate imitation of a AAA game.', 'favorite tomb raider game so far!! cool story, very fun :D', 'The gameplay felt pretty mediocre overall. Puzzles didn’t stand out in any meaningful way, and combat also felt underwhelming. The whole experience felt like a fairly standard mix of Assassin’s Creed-style systems without anything that really elevated it or made it memorable.

Progression was similarly basic. Nothing felt bad, but nothing felt especially rewarding or interesting either. It was just a very standard upgrade loop without much impact.

Graphically, the game is actually very good. The environments and presentation are strong, but for me, that only goes so far. Graphics can enhance a game, but they don’t carry it on their own.

In terms of price, it feels like a $10 game for me personally, not something I’d consider worth more than that.

Replayability is essentially nonexistent—I didn’t even finish it, and I don’t see myself going back to it.', "The overwhelming amount of plot holes in the story as well as the bugs make this game not worth recommending... I have not yet beaten the game. I am close and will see it through to its end since I made it this far, but I can't recommend it for anyone who's yet to have started it.

In regards to bugs, Lara would constantly not want to grab ledges, let go randomly from ledges, fall through geometry, and worst offender of all, some puzzles physics would break and be impossible to complete due to the frame rate being too high.

In regards to plot holes...well...

--=SPOILERS PAST THIS POINT=--
(YOU'VE BEEN WARNED)
---------------------------

99% of Trinity is mostly men, outside of Ana from Rise of the Tomb Raider. So when both Lara and Umaratu dress up as Trinity guards (or even guards of Dominguez's). Knowing this, the disguises somehow work and the guards are completely fooled.

The guards all speak with a specific accent, but with Lara in disguise speaking with an English accent, its not even remotely questioned.

At one point I was breaking Umaratu out of prison and not even 3 seconds after we left the cell, guards were yelling that she had escaped, despite not seeing her or not looking in the cell.

Combat kind of takes a backseat in this game compared to the previous 2 entries. There are still combat sections, but they're short and far and few between. Lara still has a bow, AR, shotgun, and pistol, but for a large part of the game, she's only allowed to use a bow for most of them for NO apparent reason.

---------------------------------

This is only a few of the MANY different problems with the game. The story is predictable, full of holes and not enjoyable. The gameplay is full of bugs, and with the overabundance of puzzles and traversal, and the severe lack of combat, its just not as enjoyable as its predecessor (Rise of the Tomb Raider).", '100', '10/10, great graphics and performance.', 'i survived and completed this game and managed to avoid throwing my pc out the window. wow what a ride']

name: World War 3
categories: ['Multi-player', 'PvP', 'Online PvP', 'Partial Controller Support']
short_description: World War 3 is a free-to-play tactical online multiplayer FPS where the world is your battleground. Outgun the enemy in thrilling, team-based skirmishes with a huge arsenal of weapons, vehicles, gadgets, and drones at your disposal.
detailed_description_raw:
World War 3 is an online multiplayer tactical FPS set against the backdrop of a modern global conflict. Team up to outgun and outflank the enemy in thrilling tactical skirmishes waged across real-world locations like Warsaw, Berlin, and Moscow. Customize your perfect loadout from a huge weapons arsenal, then deploy vehicles, gadgets, and drones, and call in strikes to seize the advantage.



Use every tool at your disposal to win the fight. Get your boots on the ground as an infantry soldier, drive around in tanks and vehicles, utilize drones to survey the battlefield, and call in tactical strikes for maximum damage.



Battle through stunning maps featuring realistic geography and incredible levels of detail. Take the fight from the streets of Warsaw, Berlin, Moscow, and Polyarny, to the outskirts of Smolensk and beyond.



Every shot counts. Discover realistic gameplay features that enhance immersion without impacting combat, including an advanced ballistics system, full body awareness, vehicle physics, and extensive customization.



A huge arsenal of weapons, vehicles, gadgets, drones, artillery, and airstrikes are at your disposal. Create your perfect loadout, and choose from hundreds of combinations of unique weapon components and cosmetic customizations to make it personal. Your vehicles, tactical gear, and uniforms are all fully customizable, so you can really make your mark on the battlefield.



Hit the battlefield with up to 40 players in two core game modes. Capture points in fast-paced skirmishes in Tactical Ops (20 vs. 20), and race to victory in Team Deathmatch (10 vs. 10), a classic FPS mode where you need to compete for the highest kill count!

short_description: Respawn Entertainment gives you the most advanced titan technology in its new, single player campaign & multiplayer experience. Combine & conquer with new titans & pilots, deadlier weapons, & customization and progression systems that help you and your titan flow as one unstoppable killing force.
detailed_description_raw:
Call down your Titan and get ready for an exhilarating first-person shooter experience in Titanfall® 2! The sequel introduces a new single player campaign that explores the bond between Pilot and Titan. Or blast your way through an even more innovative and intense multiplayer experience - featuring 6 new Titans, deadly new Pilot abilities, expanded customization, new maps, modes, and much more.

KEY FEATURES

  • Experience a captivating single player story. Titanfall® 2 features a single player campaign packed with action and inventive twists. Play as a Militia rifleman stranded behind enemy lines, who encounters a veteran Vanguard-class Titan. The two must work together to uphold a mission they were never meant to carry out.
  • Enjoy multiplayer action that's second to none. The sequel gives players more of the adrenaline-fueled multiplayer combat they've come to expect from the franchise. Take the fast-paced, first-person action to the next level with more Titans, deadlier Pilot abilities, and much more. And be sure to stand out in the middle of all the chaos with new, expanded Pilot, Titan and loadout personalization options!

Titanfall™ 2 Ultimate Edition Content
The best way to jump into one of the most surprising shooters of 2016 is with the Titanfall™ 2 Ultimate Edition.
Ultimate Edition includes Titanfall™ 2 base game, Deluxe Edition content (Scorch & Ion Prime Titans, Deluxe Edition Warpaint for 6 Titans, Deluxe Edition Camo for all Titans, Pilots & Weapons, Deluxe Edition Nose Arts for 6 Titans, Deluxe Edition Callsign), and Jump-Starter content (All Titans unlocked, all Pilot tacticals unlocked, 500 credits to unlock loadouts, cosmetics and gear, 10 2x XP tokens, the Underground R-201 Carbine Warpaint).

name: Shadow Warrior 2

short_description: Shadow Warrior 2 is the stunning evolution of Flying Wild Hog’s offbeat first-person shooter starring the brash warrior Lo Wang, who must again wield a devastating combination of guns, blades, magic and wit to strike down the demonic legions overwhelming the world.
detailed_description_raw:
Shadow Warrior 2 Deluxe


Shadow Warrior 2 Deluxe includes the Shadow Warrior 2 soundtrack, Shadow Warrior 2 art book, and the Solid Gold Pack containing an exclusive gold ninja, gold katana, and gold MP7 gun.


About the Game

Shadow Warrior 2 is the stunning evolution of Flying Wild Hog’s offbeat first-person shooter following the further misadventures of former corporate shogun Lo Wang. Now surviving as a reclusive mercenary on the edge of a corrupted world, the formidable warrior must again wield a devastating combination of guns, blades, magic and wit to strike down the demonic legions overwhelming the world. Battle alongside allies online in four-player co-op or go it alone in spectacular procedurally-generated landscapes to complete daring missions and collect powerful new weapons, armor, and arcane relics of legend.

STORY

Five years have passed since Lo Wang shattered the alliance between his deceitful former boss and the ancient gods of the shadow realm. Despite noble intentions, Lo Wang’s efforts to annihilate the darkness corrupted the world, creating a strange and savage new order where humans and demons live side by side.

The once feared warrior now lives in the shifting wildlands outside the reach of his enemies and the neon glow of Zilla’s cybernetic metropolis, scratching out a meager existence as a hired sword for the local Yakuza clans. When a simple mission goes wrong, Lo Wang is drawn into a volatile conflict between a brilliant young scientist, his nemesis Orochi Zilla, and the demonic forces that have become unsettled in our world. The sharp-tongued hero must once again wield lethal blades, staggering firepower, and archaic magic to purge the world of evil.

FEATURES

Blades and Bullets: Lo Wang delivers his own brand full throttle brutality with an expanded array of over 70 lethal blades and explosive firearms to overcome the demonic opposition. Become a whirlwind of steel and blood with razor sharp katana, short swords, crescent blades, and hand claws or unleash a hellish symphony of ornate firearms to decimate your enemies.

Four Player Co-Op: Battle through the expansive campaign alone or team up as a four-player typhoon of destruction online in campaign co-op mode. Tackle challenging primary missions or thrilling side quests while maintaining your own ninja style with customizable armor, items, and valuable loot from your triumphs.

Procedural Environments: The breach between the human and demon realms created an interdimensional hernia resulting in constant shifts to the world of Shadow Warrior 2. Procedurally generated landscapes and paths bring new twists and turns to once familiar terrain and routine missions.

Brutal Damage System: Choose your weapon based on the situation at hand and then dismantle everything that stands in your path with an advanced gore system. Use precision blade strikes to separate limbs and heads or switch to heavy ordinance and blow a hole right through colossal beasts.

Custom Upgrade System: Upgrade weapons in your arsenal with up to three stones at once to improve performance or augment them with devastating elemental effects. Collect karma, amulets, and armor to enhance Lo Wang’s power and shift his death-dealing artistry into overdrive.

Shadow Warrior Series

The Shadow Warrior series started back in the 90s with Shadow Warrior Classic that was remastered and released on Steam by Devolver Digital and 3D Realms as Shadow Warrior Classic Redux. Then in 2013, Flying Wild Hog and Devolver Digital released the bold reimagining of the legend of Lo Wang with the modern classic Shadow Warrior.

reviews:
["Very fun if you're into numbers shooters.
Between the combo of gems, different guns and skills, you have a lot to do, test, and run.
I've never played the multiplayer, but you may really enjoy it.", "Stupid, violent fun. Don't go in expecting a deep story; just grab a katana, slice up some demons, pick up loot, and ignore the terrible jokes. Turn your brain off and enjoy the chaos.", 'funnnnnnnnnnnnnnnn', 'way of the wang!', '7.5/10', 'This game is best.', "The first one was good, but every time I played I wanted to hit myself, BUT Shadow Warrior 2 is in my opinion top 3 hack and slash I ever played right the maps are repetitive but I think it's better like that kinda open world (free roam), haven't had so much fun playing a game lately.", 'Funny as hell. cant wait to see where 3 goes', 'FANTASTIC GAME, THE BEST ALTERNATIVE FOR DOOM, ACTUALLY BETTER THAN DOOM.', '10 years later and this game is still ABSOLUTELY ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ SPECTACULAR!!!', 'Well, I sort-of recommend this game... Its a far cry from the simple fun of the original, and this game borrowed a *lot* of gameplay from the Borderlands franchise. This means that ammo is short, you have to search for it a lot. And you *have to* find more powerful weapons and apply appropriate upgrades (there are about a 100) to be able to kill enemies later on in the game. Otherwise you simple cannot progress, as the game also warns you about.
Also, each weapon has a *cough* feature called "miss rate" where the game will let you miss your shots part of the time, even if you aimed right. Very annoying.
To skip most non-fun limitations, I played through the game with an infinite-ammo cheat using Plitch. This is the way I recommend playing this game, to get some of that original Shadow Warrior fun feeling back. :-)', "One of my old favourites from pre-Steam days.   Quick revisit for nostalgia's sake. 1.2 hours verdict: still great!", 'Great sequel and multiplayer makes it even better. Two wangs are better than one.', 'Dumb, gratuitously violent, 90% off every sale

What is there not to like?', 'Ta weno', 'This game is a blast! It’s like Doom meets Borderlands. The melee combat is incredibly satisfying, and with over 70 weapons and a deep loot system, there’s always something new to try. Huge potential, great visuals, and non-stop action. If you like slicing demons while making corny jokes, you need to play this', "Can be fun on multiplayer I guess if bought on sale.
Weapons feel very weird .. I haven't found a weapon that felt efficient or satisfying in any way.", 'What do you mean, this has Zora Neale-Hurston quotes in it? What do you mean they come out of a fortune cookie that plays a little zither jingle when you pick it up? What were the developers trying to say?

Probably nothing, actually, because there's so much random quote fill in the fortune cookies that I think they just took a list of things famous people have said and plugged them in. It's an improvement over the Confucius say jokes, but oh those are still in there too. The devs took a shaky step away from racial caricaturing and landed right in the void.

How does it play, though?

Well, the environment graphics are gorgeous. The weather effects are lovely. The soundtrack is nice background ambiance that knows how to dynamically shift tempo. A lot of Shadow Warrior (2013)'s odd design choices have been sanded down to make movement and weapon rotation and ammo management and sword comboing buttery smooth.

So, uh, predictably it's the everything else that's wrong.

Shadow Warrior 2 feels like they had a pretty decent game half made and then the CEO played Borderlands 2 and the dev team had to abruptly shift vision because the point of failure on any creative endeavor is always the C-suite. This thing wasn't made to support being a looter shooter, and it shows. The elements don't blend, the otherwise pretty tightly imagined combat is flaccid as hell, enemies are samey, the writing is trying way too hard to get that Borderlands-y ironic lilt while not at all cracking that what made BL2 work writing-wise was the genuine feeling under the surface irony.

Also. The UI.

I haven't seen it this bad in a AA. I rarely see it this bad in the indie. I thought I'd softlocked myself because of how poorly presented the dialog choice system is and while I'm an idiot I'm also not the only person who's done that. You have to double click to select dialog options. And the other menus feel kludgy and threadbare as well. As if the whole looter shooter customization system was kinda rammed in at the last minute.

Oh, and the DLC. People *loved* Borderlands 2 dlc. So how did we expand and innovate on that here?

Fight rat in alley.

This might be the worst DLC I've seen for anything. It's just random 'kill x enemy' missions. And they did two packs of it. The DLC missions are tedious and lay bare how much the combat in SW2 sucks---you just circle strafe with your blades spamming left click, and go to range when an enemy does a damage aura, and nothing really matters.

I do think that if you get this thing for free, there's some brain off fun to be had. It has a bunch of ideas that are pretty close to interesting, and the graphical execution is quite competent. However, I think you're better off playing one of the good Borderlands or any indie boomershooter. Shadow Warrior 2 doesn't really have any strengths to recommend it on.

Edit: Oh, I guess they also haven't fixed the crashing as of 2026. It's not frequent, but it also doesn't happen for any discernable reason, so you'll kinda just sometimes get booted to desktop for jumping. And  while we're talking about the movement tech, did you know that dash-spamming is faster than your sprint? Kinda doody. Also so many enemies spawn on top of each other that you're heavily incentivized to use melee weapons (which is fine, the melee combo system is interesting) but then the melee combo system doesn't seem to actually be related to your melee weapon's damage, it scales based off of the individual powers, and actually hitting anything in Shadow Warrior 2 with a melee sucks because every enemy your size has a deflect, an i-framed dodge, a flashstep, and a shove. And anything on a tether is worse because it's constantly phasing out of existence and immuning your attacks in order to phase back in two paces away in a random direction. Quantum a$$ enemy positioning. They managed to make Borderlands combat annoying. Borderlands combat is practically a flavorless meal how did you do this.

2nd Edit: Rolled credits. Fully half of my playtime was doing the stupid bounty quests. Also the plot kinda just ends. It's a short game if you don't artificially pad it. The thing that gets me, though, is that the arcs for the characters aren't actually bad. They're missing a whole bunch of middle, but I can see how they'd be solid if they had that. "We need to allow the world to change, we can't just hold on to the way we wish things had been," is a reasonable message, but it's jammed in at the last second with no real support. The writing also feels chopped. You'll get a dialog with a character where you're at each other's throats, and then the next dialog you're old friends, and then the next dialog it's like you've never met each other before. It doesn't even feel like they had the same writer do all the dialog in the same quest. And there's some wild threads that get built up and then completely dropped, or characterizations that appear out of the blue and then evaporate. Ameonna showing up to the Wang Zilla fight to be a fujoshi is weird? It's neat that the writers are talking about how they've got a more complicated dynamic than your average fps boss and hero, but it's like two minutes of her monologuing about how that must mean they're into each other, and Wang and Zilla basically don't interact at any other point during the game. And Ameonna has an extended sidequest about how someone took a video of her bathing and they're going to release it if you go after them, but then you go after them and actually it's a sex tape, and you destroy it but then actually she wanted to release it. Did three people write this without getting to look at each other's work? It wouldn't stand out so much if there was more game here, but there isn't. Again, this is like 7 hours of game and 8 hours of kill rat in alley bounty quests. And the hitboxes are scuffed. And the enemies are boring. And you get trapped in cutscene dialog hell for five minutes every time you start a quest. Play EYE Divine Cybermancy instead.', "Not so bad, but not my type.
28h for 100% (including using the glitch with getting orbs via game files).

Fair enough it could be checked as fpv shooter-slasher, but in general it's skippable.
I was not interested in the gameplay and grinding these spheres, karma, guns and so on.
Story is so weird too. At the same time bright and unique.

I'd like to look in the eyes of the developer who created 10k orbs achievement. Retarded stuff, absolutely not balanced.", "ok so far. once again a checkpoint game which is stupid. checkpoints are fairly close so why not just give us the option to save anywhere!?! really pathetic mentality of the dev community. and being unable to alt+tab out of the game is pretty poor. who are these devs!?!

story is pretty basic along with the graphics. pretty much the same as #1.. but with plenty of bugs.. mission items that 'land' (as a result of a kill) outside of the playable area, so you cant retrieve it! mission objedtives that dont spawn (in the optional missions), all too often the mission path takes you to the most to the opposide of the map and then back again! useless devs.. skills that claim a particular 'action' that doesnt work, physics doesnt seem to play a part in this game, as enemy that are large take up the exact same space along with many small enemy...just to be able to be in 'reach' of you (to trample. hit and maim), many other issues. the character models are pretty poorly rendered and animated. the combat is pretty lame.. I'm not sure whether I do get affected by environmental explosions or not.. but the enemy cant impact these barrels, but they get affected by the explosions, and I dont appear to.. very poor. I'm playing on normal but the enemy hardly damage me yet I'm getting 'hammered' on all sides.. its just chaos for chaos sake.. it's not to say I havent 'died', but mostly due to other reasons not combat. falling off ledges into non playable areas, etc.

it's bordering on 'not recommended'.. because of its uninspired game play.. I found myself actively avoiding combat (via vanish) just to speed up game play. it's not like you dont already level up fast enough without the constant battles to hinder your movement. its 'fun' enough if you get it on the cheap", 'hell yeah', 'So, I recommend this game, with a major caveat: This is not a boom-shoot, even in the modern sense like 2013. It's not even an arena shooter like 3. We're in full-on shlooter. And because of that, 2's hard to compare to the rest of the Trilogy, because while they're all shooting to be different things, 2 is trying to be *really* different. This...isn't necessarily a bad thing, at least to me (somebody who's put a lot of time into Borderlands 1, 2, and Pre-Sequel), but it's WEIRD, especially within the context of trying to make a cohesive series of games.

So the big positive: the movement is the best in the trilogy. A good jump doubled, a decent speed, no stamina limits (which admittedly renders the sprint pointless since you can endlessly dash), no jank-ass mid-00s fall damage, it just feels good to move around, and unlike, say, Borderlands (1, 2, and PS), the movement is great right off the bat, rather than needing to level up into it.

Unlike a lot of shlooters, the weapon pool is fixed: there are a set number of weapons (three or four to a category, with categories including pistols, pump shotguns, sawed-offs, assault rifles claws, swords, heavy swords, paired short blade, railguns, grenade launchers, rockets, etc.), usually with one unique trait/stat, and the "Loot" comes into play with randomly generated gems that you can slot into these fixed weapons. If nothing else, this system lets you stick to a weapon you like, rather than it falling behind, though it's easier to get overwhelmed by obtuse choices, especially without character classes to actually synergize.

Weapon feedback is, at least to me about how it was in 2013 (which is to say, not great, in my opinion). I dunno if it's because of the introduction of the shlooter elements (enemy buffs, elemental weaknesses/resistances, variable weapon stats), or if it's still just kinda janky, but at the very least, the enemies' tankiness feels explained, and in endgame you can usually subvert it by setting up something busted to make them die fast, even if the feeling between "hit" and "death" doesn't feel quite right.

If the maps themselves are proc-gen (as even the advertising suggests), I haven't noticed; the only thing that feels variable is enemy spawns. Maps seem to be reused wholecloth, not just in pieces like proc-gen normally does. The missions just pick a start and endpoint somewhere on the map. On the one hand, the world does feel cohesive, like it exists outside just the bounds of a single mission, but on the other, the path doesn't always feel like there's any progression, since there aren't any real environmental setpieces.

The game looks pretty. Stylized in much the same way that 2013 was (but with about 3 years extra polygons), but the environments are a lot less grounded. This could be a good thing or a bad thing. I personally don't hate it. If nothing else, the game seems to run better than its predescessor.

The story is mostly fine. You've got a new voice in your head who isn't as much fun as Hoji from 2013, but they get some surprisingly funny moments. Several of the supporting cast is back from 2013 as well, and fleshed out some. The biggest lurch is going to be piecing together how the status quo shifted in between 2013 and 2, because that's only addressed in very specific ways, rather than the broad scope. Even writing this, having beaten the game 3 times (but not having read through the logs, pending getting all of them so I'm not missing pieces), I still don't know what actually *caused* "The Collision", only how certain entities/characters handled it when it did. And the ending is...abrupt. Like, really abrupt.

And for whatever reason, mission progress is not saved for clients in co-op, so if you're going through with friends, one person is going to have to commit to being the host. You do keep all your loot/skills/etc., so no need to worry about that.

So yeah, this is a really weird diversion for the series, that you'll either like or hate depending on how much of a genre-purist you are. It's smoothed out a lot of stuff from 2013, but the structure itself probably is less appealing. Actually having "high-mobility" and "co-op" in the same game, in a story where that doesn't undercut the tone (looking at you F.3.A.R.) is nice.', 'I just can't get into it, there's so much ♥♥♥♥ going on, and the weapons feel awful, the overwhelming amount of updates and pointless crap to collect are just exhausting.

If they made the weapon collecting like in Borderlands, that would've been one problem solved, but instead you get thousands of weapon "upgrades", and it's just annoying.

All of this I could handle, but the generated levels just feel bad to play through.

', 'Shadow Warrior 2 is one of the most fun and chaotic action games I’ve played. The combat is incredibly satisfying, mixing fast-paced gunplay with brutal melee attacks. Using katanas, chainsaws, shotguns, and explosive weapons against waves of demons never gets boring. The movement also feels smooth with dashes and double jumps that make every fight intense and stylish.

What makes the game even better is the variety of weapons, upgrades, and loot. There are so many different ways to play, and customizing your loadout keeps the gameplay fresh. The co-op mode is also a big plus and makes the action even more enjoyable with friends.

The story and humor may not be for everyone, but the gameplay easily carries the experience. If you enjoy fast FPS combat, lots of gore, and nonstop action, Shadow Warrior 2 is definitely worth playing.', "Multiplayer is trash. I'm unable to recommend it, however, for a few reasons. The most obvious issue with the game right away is its lack of players. I'm still seeing some real people pop up here and there, but it's mostly a dead game (though doing a lot better than the original release of Day Of Defeat from 2003). The game tries to preserve some sense of normalcy with all the bots running around every map, which almost makes it feel like you're in an authentic match. And yet, these bots are horrid. They turn a corner in the blink of an eye and one-shot you before you can even comprehend the colour of their uniform. I don't know why they had to be designed with Terminator vision, but it's extremely unenjoyable. I guess that's why they didn't call it Day Of Victory, hur hur. Another Source remaster, the highly popular Counter-Strike Source, allowed you to create matches with bots of your own and even let you tweak their difficulty. No such luck here. 

Another thing that never ceases to bother me is how boring multiplayer games are. See, Day Of Defeat Source has really nice graphics and neat gunplay, offering a wide variety of different weapons in the same style as Team Fortress 2, but all this does is make me crave a single player experience that much more. I was actually surprised that enemy players do die from a more realistic amount of bullets, more akin to the style of Call Of Duty, so I found that quite nice for a Valve game. The environments are really well-detailed, and the Source engine lends its hand to a lot of satisfying physics to toy around with. name: Tropico 5
categories: ['Single-player', 'Multi-player', 'Co-op', 'Cross-Platform Multiplayer', 'Steam Achievements', 'Steam Trading Cards', 'Steam Workshop', 'Steam Cloud', 'Includes level editor', 'Family Sharing']
short_description: Return to the remote island nation of Tropico and expand your Dynasty’s reign from the early colonial period to beyond the 21st Century, facing new challenges including advanced trading mechanics, technology and scientific research, as well as cooperative and competitive MULTIPLAYER.
detailed_description_raw:
Return to the remote island nation of Tropico in the next installment of the critically acclaimed and hugely popular ‘dictator sim’ series. Expand your Dynasty’s reign from the early colonial period to beyond the 21st Century, facing an all-new set of challenges, including advanced trading mechanics, technology and scientific research, exploration and for the first time in Tropico history – cooperative and competitive MULTIPLAYER for up to 4 players.

    The Eras - Start your reign during colonial times, survive the World Wars and the Great Depression, be a dictator during the Cold War, and advance your country to modern times and beyond. From the 19th century to the 21st, each era carries its own challenges and opportunities.
    The Dynasty - Each member of El Presidente’s extended family is present on the island and may be appointed as a ruler, a manager, an ambassador or a general. Invest in the members of your Dynasty to unlock new traits and turn them into your most valuable assets.
    Research and Renovate - Advance your nation by discovering new buildings, technologies and resources. Renovate your old buildings to more efficient modern buildings.
    Advanced trade system and trade fleet - Amass a global trade fleet and use your ships to secure trade routes to neighboring islands or world superpowers, both for export and import.
    Explore your island - Discover what lies beyond the fog of war. Find valuable resource deposits and explore the ruins of ancient civilizations.
    All new art - All artwork has been re-designed from scratch to provide Tropico 5 with a unique visual identity. Choose from over 100 buildings from each of the individual eras.
    Cooperative and competitive multiplayer – Up to 4 players can build up their own cities and economies on any given island map. Players can choose to share resources, supplies and population or declare war on each other.

The game is a decent base, but soon you see a DLC that sort of adds a bit here and there, this is the 5th version yet there is 12 DLC packs, is this addition or simply taking the piss and you for the ride ?

Its getting Paradox levels of DLC stupid, you decide if you want to keep paying for content updates, again the base game is good but the devs keep going paid DLC heavy,', 'Churches make a loot more money then anything', 'This game allows for casual, low stress problem solving. Can't wait for 7s release!", "I finished this one almost 10 years ago on my old MacBook Pro - didn't have any issues whatsoever. Graphics might be a bit dated now, but I really don't care. Gameplay is fun, soundtrack is great, story a bit cheesy with a lot of puns and jokes :) Recommended for every wannabe dictator :D", "Got Achievements for how good I was doing but still random rebels popped up and homeless people literally didn't want to move into free houses, it feels like the game will force bad events even if you're managing everything well

Approval seems to artificially go down during elections, even if the average Caribbean satisfaction remains the same your approval will go down for no apparent good reason, same stats, 20% less approval.

You can be launching space rockets from your tiny uber wealthy island and still people with AK's will try to kill you even if they're living in mansions.", 'Still great!', '[h1]🌍 [i]Tropico 5[/i] - El Presidente Never Retires[/h1]

[b]What I Played:[/b] I've been running island nations since the original Tropico back in the day, and honestly this series has been a constant in my gaming life. 66 hours into Tropico 5 and I'm still finding reasons to start "just one more mandate." This time around I played through the entire era progression from colonial times all the way to modern day, ran a few multiplayer sessions with a buddy, and spent way too long in the level editor pretending I'm some kind of Caribbean urban planner.

[b]The Good:[/b]
[list]
[*]The era system is genuinely the best addition to the franchise. Going from colonial survival to cold war political juggling to modern tourism empires gives every playthrough this great sense of progression that earlier Tropicos never had. As someone who started from Tropico 1, watching the series evolve to this point is kind of beautiful.
[*]Dynasty system adds real personality. Having your family members govern different buildings and level up over generations makes you actually care about your ruling bloodline. My minister of defense has been carrying my regime since the World Wars era.
[*]The multiplayer co-op is surprisingly fun. Building neighboring islands with a friend and trading resources back and forth scratches an itch I didn't know I had in a city builder.
[*]Still has that classic Tropico humor. The radio announcements, the ridiculous election speeches, the citizen bios — it's all there and it still makes me laugh after all these hours.
[/list]

[b]The Bad:[/b]
[list]
[*]Combat is clunky and feels tacked on. Military conflicts boil down to "build more army bases and hope for the best." It's the weakest part of an otherwise solid game.
[*]The AI factions can be annoyingly binary sometimes. You're either their best friend or public enemy number one, not much nuance in between. Veterans of the older games will notice the diplomacy feels simplified.
[/list]

[b]Verdict:[/b] [b]Recommended[/b] - Five games deep into this series and I'm still hooked. Tropico 5 isn't perfect, but it's the most accessible and feature-rich entry for newcomers while still having enough depth to keep longtime fans like me building one more plantation at 2 AM. El Presidente approves.', "This one works in Linux too. So it's cool to be the most corrupted dictator ever as long as it's in a game.", 'There are parts of this game, especially the Dynasties and upgrading El Pres & Fam, that make this my favorite tropico of all. It does have the occasional bug (like WSAD seems to stop working to move the cam), but is ultimately great. Tropico 6 has a far improved Political Almanac and UI. This is definitely one of my favorite games of all time.', 'good strategy game', 'best tropico ever
', 'EL PRESIDENTE BEST PRESIDENTE', "Great game, shouldn't have skipped from 4 to 6. Really makes 6 look worse by comparison. This game feels finished whereas 6 feels lazy and rushed.", 'Positive review', 'Nice game, with good humor and interesting economy building.', "Tropico 5 is a decent city-builder with a great satirical sense of humor about politics and dictatorship, but it's hard not to feel like a step down from Tropico 4 in almost every way that matters. The era system is an interesting idea that ends up locking you out of buildings that should be available much earlier, and the dynasty mechanic adds busywork without the deep character customization the previous game offered. Late-game becomes a frustrating mess of unexplained unhappiness, rebels you can't seem to quell, and economic death spirals with no real diagnostic tools to help you figure out what went wrong. That said, the colonial era is genuinely charming, the writing still gets a laugh, and there's enough here to sink a solid 20-30 hours into if you're new to the series. If you've played Tropico 4, though, you'll likely spend most of your time missing it.", 'gg', 'whatever you do there alwais rebellion', '**APP REVIEW BY DONALD J. TRUMP FOR TROPICO 5**

Everyone is talking about Tropico 5. They come up to me, the best game players, tremendous players, they say, “Sir, have you played it?” And I have. I’ve played it. And let me tell you, it’s a game. A big game. But it’s got problems. **HUGE** problems. But also some good things. Very good.

First, the good. When you’re El Presidente—and you should be, because the other candidates are low-energy—you rule an island. A beautiful island. And the people? They’re alive! Not like other games, where the people are dummies. Sleepy Joe’s game would have dummy people. In MY game, Tropico 5, they have OPINIONS. They complain about housing, they get mad about wages—always complaining, very ungrateful!—and they even have factions. You build a factory, they want a church. You build a ten-star hotel, the communists get upset. **SAD!** It’s a living, breathing society. It’s like running a real country, but without the fake news media. A tremendous simulation. Maybe the best. Everyone says so.

But here’s the bad. The DLC. A total scam. A con job by the globalists at Kalypso. They take features that should be in the beautiful, perfect base game—which I paid for, a lot of money, believe me—and they lock them away. It’s a disgrace! We should build a wall around that DLC policy and make Kalypso pay for it. **UNACCEPTABLE.**

And the “eras”? They told us, “Trump—I mean, El Presidente—you go through time, from colonial times to modern times! It’s HUGE!” Well, it’s not huge. It’s basic. Very basic. You get some new buildings, a little technology. It doesn’t change the game. It’s the same game, with a different hat. Not a great hat. A bad hat. Low energy.

Also, the jokes? Not funny. Tropico used to be funnier. Sharper. This one? It’s trying too hard. Like a comedian who’s lost his fastball. And the interface? A disaster. Very confusing. Not intuitive. You need to be a rocket scientist to figure out where the edicts are. I like simple. Big buttons. Beautiful buttons. This is not that.

So, the verdict? It’s a mixed bag. A very mixed bag. A tremendous simulation of a living society—maybe the best—ruined by greedy DLC, weak new features, bad jokes, and a confusing interface. It could have been a perfect ten. A home run. But they got lazy. Or maybe they were interfered with. Who knows?

**FINAL RATING: 6 out of 10. COULD BE GREAT AGAIN, BUT RIGHT NOW, IT’S JUST OKAY.**', 'Forces you to play with a controller; KBM is borderline non-functional, with no visible way to change settings', "You don't win, you just do a little better each time.", 'thats why i hate strategies. i spent like 4 hours building colony. i grew to like it. and then bcs of miscalculation i watched it being destroed for 10 mins. i dont want to play games for a while', ':)']

categories: ['Multi-player', 'PvP', 'Online PvP', 'LAN PvP', 'Cross-Platform Multiplayer', 'Steam Achievements', 'Full controller support', 'In-App Purchases', 'Steam Cloud', 'Includes level editor', 'Remote Play on TV']
short_description: From one of gaming's most iconic sagas, Halo is bigger than ever. Featuring an expansive open-world campaign and a dynamic free to play multiplayer experience.
detailed_description_raw:
Campaign:


When all hope is lost and humanity’s fate hangs in the balance, the Master Chief is ready to confront the most ruthless foe he’s ever faced. Step inside the armor of humanity’s greatest hero to experience an epic open-world adventure and explore the massive scale of the Halo ring.

With Network Campaign Co-Op and Mission Replay, you can relive your favorite moments, rediscover collectibles and explore the wonders of Zeta Halo on your own or with up to three other fireteam members.

To experience the campaign, purchase Halo Infinite (Campaign).

Free-to-Play Multiplayer:


Experience Halo’s celebrated multiplayer reimagined and free-to-play! With over 70 maps in matchmaking, billions of customization configurations, and countless community Forge creations to experience, Halo Infinite is the franchise’s most expansive multiplayer offering to date.

Enjoy an unprecedented variety of gameplay experiences, ranging from fast-paced intense ranked modes to whimsical mayhem in Custom Games, or even co-operative coordination in the wave-based survival mode Firefight: King of the Hill. Assemble your Fireteam and conquer every mode – Halo offers endless opportunities for teamwork, strategy, and unforgettable victories.

Forge Beta:


Halo’s legendary content creation tool is more powerful than ever with advanced features like a sophisticated visual scripting engine, mode editor, object scaling, lighting and audio tools as well as marked improvements in fidelity and object budget limits.

Forge also includes the powerful AI Toolkit, allowing players to add campaign AI to their creations. Whether it’s remaking iconic experiences from previous Halo entries or creating something entirely unique, the possibilities for thrilling custom maps and game modes are infinite. "

reviews:
['Halo is dead', 'The best 343 Halo game. Feels like a mix between the OG and the newer games', '', 'for reach', 'very enjoyable halo experience', 'game completely blocks sign in attempts to my microsoft account this product is now unplayable', 'didnt play halo since i was a kid and this is so much fun, muliplayer is awesome and the campaign was amazing, good story and long but fun the whole way', "After linking my Xbox account, I completely forgot that I had already finished Halo Infinite back when it first launched on Xbox Game Pass. As soon as I started the game, I suddenly unlocked 26 achievements all at once. Even so, because I wanted to complete every Halo game in order, I decided to replay the full campaign.

One small annoyance is that the game always loads into multiplayer first, and then you have to switch over to the campaign.

The game opens with Atriox fighting Master Chief and throwing him out into space. Later, Master Chief is rescued by a Pelican pilot, which sets up the story well. Halo Infinite is the first Halo game I played that loaded shaders at start up. It was also the first game in the series that randomly closed to desktop a couple of times during my 18 hours of gameplay.

Thankfully, there was no real damage done because I could just reload and continue without losing much progress.
This is the first Halo game that really becomes open world.

Even after finishing the campaign, you can continue exploring the map and completing activities. Somehow, the graphics continue to improve again and look fantastic.

It is definitely worth playing Halo Wars 2 beforehand because it gives important background on Atriox and the Banished.

Ammo types are now much more clearly defined.

Covenant enemies with shields now move their shields around to block the same weak point, which makes them tougher and more annoying in a good way.

Suit upgrades are introduced, but I mostly relied on the health upgrades and the grapple. The grapple quickly becomes one of the most useful and fun tools in the game.

Power Seeds play a major role in opening doors and powering different systems. They are basically batteries, but they work well as a gameplay mechanic.

New weapons are introduced, such as the Disruptor.

It quickly becomes clear that the story takes place after Atriox's apparent death and that Escharum is now leading the Banished.

Mini-bosses can now be tracked down and defeated. These enemies are much tougher than regular troops and are usually important Banished members.

I had one issue during the Rescue the Pilot mission where the waypoint was not in the correct place. I had to go southwest from the marked location to find the Pelican and continue the mission. A quick search online showed I was definitely not the only person with this problem, and apparently it was never fixed. Even with a few technical issues, Halo Infinite is still a great experience.

The open world design, improved graphics, new enemies, and new gameplay systems make it feel fresh. The ending is definitely a cliff hanger, which I expected. However, it left too many unanswered questions, and with no new Halo game coming soon it felt a bit hollow. Even so, Halo Infinite was a lot of fun and a very enjoyable way to continue Master Chief's story.", 'meh', 'lowk mid', 'Goat game uncapped Frames', 'I love halo, this game was rough. short_description: Satisfactory is a first-person open-world factory building game with a dash of exploration and combat. Play alone or with friends, explore an alien planet, create multi-story factories, and enter conveyor belt heaven!
detailed_description_raw:
Satisfactory is a first-person open-world factory building game with a dash of exploration and combat. Play alone or with friends, explore an alien planet, create multi-story factories, and enter conveyor belt heaven!

Construct

Conquer nature by building massive factories across the land. Expand wherever and however you want. The planet is filled with valuable natural resources just waiting to be utilized. As an employee of FICSIT it’s your duty to make sure they come to good use.

Automate

Construct your factories with gracious perfection or build intricate webs of conveyor belts to supply all your needs. Automate trucks and trains to reach your faraway outposts and be sure to handle liquids properly by transporting them in pipes. It’s all about minimizing manual labour!

Explore & Exploit

Venture on expeditions to search for new materials and be sure to put everything to good use. Nature is yours to harvest! You have vehicles, jetpacks, jump pads and more at your disposal to make the exploration easier. Equip the proper safety gear as well, just in case you run into the local wildlife.

FEATURES

• Open World: Explore the huge (30km2) alien planet that is Massage-2(AB)b with its unique fauna and creatures.
• Co-Op: Build a factory yourself or share the joy with your friends. Up to you!
• Factory Building: Experience building a huge factory from a first-person perspective. Automate and optimize it to perfection for your personal satisfaction.
• Customization: Customize your factory to your own liking. Build at high altitudes or over wide plains, there is almost no limits in the making of your tailor-made factory.
• Vehicles: Travel the world with class. Use jump pads, factory carts, jetpacks, hypertubes, trucks or trains. The choice is yours!

', 'crack', 'Satisfactory is about being dropped onto an alien planet with a mission: find resources and build factories to automate production. The execution is strong, with few bugs and an appealing presentation that keeps most players hooked for hours. There are very few stress-inducing factors in the game; instead, the gameplay is cleverly designed to keep you busy for long sessions. The more you fix, the more you find that needs fixing—and just as frustration reaches a critical level, the game perfectly times a new tier of upgrades to relieve the pressure.

Achieving smooth gameplay requires a great deal of planning, routing, and calculating flows between machines. You must ensure that input and output match so that belts neither starve nor clog, as a single bottleneck can ruin the productivity of an entire facility. It takes many hours to learn how to operate optimally, which almost guarantees you will be rebuilding your factories more than once. As the game progresses, part requirements shift, forcing constant changes to your pipelines.

The implementation quality is high, leaving the impression that things just "work," though there are areas the developers can still refine. Many aspects offer a more stable version of the settlement building found in Fallout 4, albeit with different trade-offs. For example, stronger grid snapping reduces "glitches" but also limits some of the creative freedom present in the latter.

Interestingly, there are no rewards for aesthetics. This creates an unexpected psychological challenge for those with (mild) OCD: you are forced to decide whether to accept something "good enough" in the name of progress or get stuck indefinitely trying to build the perfect layout.

Overall, the game is very enjoyable, but still not perfect. The UI is good, but not great. The engine can feel somewhat soulless; many objects don't obey the laws of physics, making surfaces feel as hard as diamond. This is partially offset by the excellent sound design—for instance, the satisfaction of hitting a glass window while flying with the jetpack. However, interacting with the environment can be hit or miss.

Finally, there are several areas that are barely acceptable:

1. No Linux/SteamOS support.

2. Steam feels like an afterthought. The game feels as though it was designed for another platform and later integrated into Steam for convenience. Furthermore, the developers don't seem to interact much with the Steam Community; suggestions and bug reports are directed to an external Q&A site, requiring yet another registration. It feels counterproductive to ask players to exert extra effort to help improve a product they are already actively supporting.', "So, just under 48 hours ago I bought this game for myself and a friend... and I've already got over 22 hours logged on it...
I don't know if I can give it a higher recommendation!", 'ts is so peak bro, i tear down an amazing alien planet and release toxic factory fumes into the enviroment', "Fentanyl is less addictive than this ⚠️wouldn't recommend⚠️", "If Factorio is cocaine, this game is meth. That's about all I can say.", 'This game is so satisfying to play. The saddest part is that the game runs fine for the first major part of the game, trapping you and making you think it's a genuinely well-made game. However, once you start to put real hours into the game, it immediately falls apart. The first glaring error I noticed was that the hoverpack straight-up doesn't work. Power drains from it whether you're actually using it or not, and depending on how fast you're going, it drains fast. There's an extremely annoying bug that makes you infinitely fly upwards unless you press the spacebar again which thankfully stops it. The glider part of it won't turn on after it's drained of power and you use it to glide to the ground once, whereas you'd think it should also have the capacity to glide. Even if these are somehow features, they'd be considered ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ ones. Then there's the crashes. If you have a big enough factory, which is the entire purpose of the game, the game will never open again. Instead, upon the beginning of rendering the world after you load in, it'll crash for some reason or another. And if it doesn't, it'll crash 10, 20, 30, 60 minutes later when you're in the middle of something else. This game was not designed with sustainability in mind. It's a trap for new players to get invested after spending real money (FORTY ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ DOLLARS FOR THIS PIECE OF ♥♥♥♥?! I can't believe I spent that much!) for the IDEA of a factory-building simulator which implodes on itself the moment you start actually having fun. To put this in perspective, there is another ♥♥♥♥♥♥ $40 indie game in 7 Days to Die which I would genuinely rather be forced to play for the rest of my life than having to load this game again.", 'Best game i have ever got!!!!!', 'this game is amazing You can build however you like and make some amazing builds i play this alot and i love it i Recommend to anyone.']

short_description: Ride and fight into a deadly, post pandemic America. Play as Deacon St. John, a drifter and bounty hunter who rides the broken road, fighting to survive while searching for a reason to live in this open-world action-adventure game.
detailed_description_raw:
Days Gone is an open-world action-adventure game set in a harsh wilderness two years after a devastating global pandemic.

Step into the dirt flecked shoes of former outlaw biker Deacon St. John, a bounty hunter trying to find a reason to live in a land surrounded by death. Scavenge through abandoned settlements for equipment to craft valuable items and weapons, or take your chances with other survivors trying to eke out a living through fair trade… or more violent means.

KEY FEATURES

• A striking setting: From forests and meadows, to snowy plains and desert lava fields, the Pacific Northwest is both beautiful and lethal. Explore a variety of mountains, caves, mines and small rural towns, scarred by millions of years of volcanic activity.

• Brutal encounters: With vicious gangs and hordes of Freakers roaming the land, you’ll need to make full use of a variety of customizable traps, weapons, and upgradable skills to stay alive. Don’t forget your Drifter bike, an invaluable tool in a vast land.

• An ever-changing environment: Jump on the saddle of Deacon’s trusty motorbike and explore a dynamic world dramatically affected by the weather, a dramatic day/night cycle and the evolving Freakers, who adjust to their surroundings – and the people in it.

• A compelling story: Lose yourself in a powerful tale of desperation, betrayal and regret, as Deacon St. John searches for hope after suffering a deep, personal loss. What makes us human when faced with the daily struggle for survival?

INCLUDES

• New Game Plus

• Survival Mode

• Challenge Mode

• Bike Skins

PC features include ultra-wide monitor support, unlocked framerates and improved graphics (increased level of details, field of view, foliage draw distances).

reviews:
["Loved the game's vibes. Zombies, Bikes, Forest, Mountains... This game was created by nature lovers. The sunrise and sunset feels real, like memories from the past at the countryside. Couple of things I'd add or fix:

1) You can't salvage or sell other's bikes. It would be cool if you'd get special upgrades or extra credits from salvaged bikes.

2) Scripted dialogues sometimes blends with others. You've got too many calls at the same time, while main character is delivering the line after spotting bandits.

3) Some missions are pointless. You go to check on one guy traveling the half of the map, he says that he's fine and that's it. I wish there were more opportunities to interract with side characters. Something like in Mass Effect - you get to know them better, you'll get a stronger motivated team.

I'd rate this game 8/10. I am enjoying it even just driving.", "One of the best apocolyptic game I've ever played
", "This game is a 6/10 for me. Bought it on sale, played a couple of hours and hated it. I was past the refund point, so I uninstalled it to make room for something else. Fast forward a year or more, and I wanted to go through my steam library, settling on this first. Progression in the early chapters sucks, but after getting some bike upgrades and some skills, I finally got into the gameplay loop. However for a game that puts so much emphasis on your bike, vehicle combat might as well be non-existent. The Hordes are fun though, and mid to late game guns feel great. Story is bad to average for the entirety of it. Main character is depressed biker that jokes to cope with the world. The MC could of been endearing, but most of the time it comes off as one note or him being a jerk for no reason. Storylines are left unfinished. Characters randomly stop being relevant or are completely forgotten (O'Brian especially. Nero storyline as whole really). Gameplay bugs and graphical bugs are present even after the 5 years this game has been out on PC. At the end of the game, I had store keepers sitting in invisible stalls with invisible walls, making it annoying to figure out how to interact with them.", "One of the best games I've ever played. It hurts my soul that I will never get to play it for the first time again. I cannot believe there is not a sequel. 

All that aside I've played and beat it twice and after you beat it the world feels kind of empty and there's not really any challenge since you're all beefed up at that point. Even on my second play through I had almost all the unlocks and special stuff shortly after unlocking the crater lake area. It was nice to play through and not struggle like my first time. 

I can't help but think if Bend studio retooled this game into an extraction shooter it would go stupid hard and be a richer experience than co op alone. Such a shame this is a stand alone game.", 'Fire', 'like', 'Motorcycles, Guns, Zombies and a good story.
needs a sequel.', 'Days Gone is a game that’s better than its reputation—but still flawed in ways that matter.

At its core, it’s an open-world survival game with strong atmosphere. The Pacific Northwest setting is genuinely beautiful, and riding your motorcycle through forests, rainstorms, and abandoned roads feels immersive. The bike itself is a standout mechanic—you’re constantly managing fuel, repairs, and upgrades, which adds tension and makes traversal meaningful.

The highlight, though, is the Horde system. Massive swarms of Freakers create some of the most intense moments in the game. Planning your approach, setting traps, and barely surviving chaotic encounters is where Days Gone truly shines.

That said, the game struggles with pacing. The story starts slow—too slow—and takes hours before it really hooks you. Deacon as a protagonist can feel repetitive and overly gruff at first, though he becomes more likable over time. Mission design also gets repetitive: ride somewhere, clear enemies, repeat.

Combat is solid but not exceptional. Shooting and stealth work fine, but nothing feels particularly innovative. Early game frustration is common due to limited resources and weaker weapons, though it improves as you progress.

Technically, it had a rough launch, but most major issues have been patched. Still, occasional bugs and clunky AI can break immersion.

Overall, Days Gone is a slow-burn experience. If you’re patient, it rewards you with great atmosphere and memorable large-scale encounters. If you’re not, its repetitive structure and pacing issues might wear you down.', 'Waiting on days gone 2 if there is one', 'i found the game to be quite calming to play apart from the hoards its just nice to be able to ride around on your bike taking in views that you may never see in real life the game play and mission are also quite engaging and feel smooth unlike some other rpgs out there 
', "Sometimes, I feel like Deacon St. John and Boozer is on drugs or something when talking or maybe that's just how they talk. But da*m, i couldn't get enough of Boozer's half a** joke. hA Ha hA. yeah only half- but anyways, the game is good with some minor bugs sometimes that's not really a big deal.  And riding a bike was awesome!", 'Sometimes crashes but manageable', 'Is a nice game, a kind of RDR with motorcycle and swarms of zombies', 'yes', 'Great game mechanics, Great story, totally worth picking up.', 'lots of action.', 'what an underrated gem this game is, good story, good gameplay and a sick horde system. really fun game highly recommend', 'One of the best zombie games I ever played. Is a shame we wont have sequel.', 'Amazing gameplay. The story can feel a little corny, but I liked it', 'peak from start to finish', 'Now I understand why people got tired of open-world games.

This game sat in my backlog since the PS4 era, and I finally decided to give it a chance. After 20+ hours, I completely understand why the market got burned out on the early 2020s open-world formula.

The Bad:
The Story & Writing: The plot is incredibly bland. Deacon (the protagonist) talks nonsense 90% of the time. There is nothing like trying to be stealthy while he screams "GOT IT!" at the top of his lungs while opening a door.

Narrative Bloat: There are 10–15 different story branches, but only one makes more sense than a "Baby Shark" video. Even the main quests feel like a never-ending cycle of fetch missions.

Useless Upgrades: The progression is frustrating. Your third fuel tank upgrade still only gives you enough gas to travel about 3 miles before you're empty again.

Bullet Sponges: The scaling is bizarre. A bear can eat 100 MG rounds to the torso, and some random human enemies will survive three grenades to the chest.

Gunplay: Every weapon feels "samey." There’s no realistic recoil or bullet physics. Deacon has the accuracy of a world-class marine sniper even while standing still after a 2-mile sprint with three guns on his back. The scopes don't even sway.

The Good:
Visuals: Despite its age, the game still looks great. The world feels empty, but the atmosphere and lighting are solid.

Bike Mechanics: Riding the bike feels good—when you actually have fuel in the tank for more than five minutes.

The Hordes: Seeing the hordes for the first time is amazing, and fighting them is easily the highlight of the game.

Conclusion:
This game feels like it was designed specifically for people who 100% completed Assassin's Creed Valhalla and still thought doing the same repetitive tasks for 200 hours was "peak fun."

Final Verdict: 5/10. Play it if you have nothing else to do, but don't expect a masterpiece.', ' A shame this isnt getting a sequel. These types of games are rare and I love it', 'i bought this game a time ago tried to play it got halfway through and the game errored my save so i quit it came back and im trying to beat it, this game has some of the worst everything i have ever seen the game is riddled with bugs, ai pathing wont work, you die from random heights, and the game wont load the maps so you have to quit it and loadback in and i still might not fix it

It's ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ annoying, and you can't turn it off or mod it away. The game also installs Sony spyware onto your PC, so there's that.", 'First things first: did I enjoy the game?

Days Gone is like The Walking Dead and Red Dead Redemption having a baby, developed by Ubisoft. Only without the well written characters or the charismatic protagonist.
The gameplay and the gameplay loop are fun. Otherwise, I probably wouldn’t have finished the game. But I didn’t really like any of the characters, and the story is so incredibly boring. I waited until the end for the big payoff and didn’t get it. So if I’d skipped all the dialogue and cutscenes, I would have had just as much fun. My biggest problem, though, is really that Deacon just can’t keep his mouth shut. No matter how quietly I sneak through a Marauder camp, Deacon always has to shout, “Well, how do you like being on the other end of the gun?!” and that with every single kill… which then killed my mood all over again.
With that in mind, the €13 I paid for it was definitely worth it, but in the end I’m glad I’ve finished the game. I’m not going to put myself through that again.

name: Strobophagia | Rave Horror
categories: ['Single-player', 'Steam Achievements', 'Full controller support', 'Steam Cloud', 'Family Sharing']
short_description: An occult forest rave ⸸ Strange rituals ⸸ Enigmatic organisers ⸸ An ancient evil ⸸ Pure Psychedelic Horror
detailed_description_raw:
Dance, Drink and Die




Following a promise of the ultimate expression of freedom, you find yourself at the Headless Rave Festival. However, that freedom comes at a cost as the organisers announce that the price of admission is the lives of all attendees.

Find a way to escape, even as the forest itself appears to work against you. Try to maintain a grip on reality while logic takes a backseat. And whatever you do, stay away from the sinister presence in the darkness.

Can you make your way out without losing yourself?


  • Classic survival horror through a psychedelic rave lens
  • Macabre puzzles following twisted logic
  • In-game smartphone mechanic that helps you locate points of interest and consult the numerous attendees for help

However, that freedom comes at a cost as the organisers announce that the price of admission is the lives of all attendees. reviews:
['Great style lacking in substance', "I enjoyed this game quite a bit! The atmosphere was fascinating and the entire idea had me enthralled. Unfortunately I can only recommend purchasing when on sale due to some quality of life improvements that are quite important for replayability such as checkpoints to go back to when you reach a certain ending. However I do commend just how adventurous this take on a horror title is and don't regret purchasing it.", "It's so short. I want to like it however there is very little replayability (and for its price that's not good). I beat it in the first go in under 2 hours with no previous knowledge of the game and so much loitering. The rest of the time I just spent slapping people to rave music.", 'DANG THIS GAME IS PEAK, i love it sooooo much', 'Strobophagia | Rave Horror, developed by Green Tile Digital and published by Green Tile Digital alongside THQ Nordic, is a strikingly unconventional horror experience that deliberately breaks away from the genre’s usual darkness and restraint. Instead of abandoned houses, hospitals, or fog-filled streets, the game drops players into a surreal rave festival deep in the forest, where pulsing neon lights, masked dancers, and electronic music form the backdrop for an unsettling descent into the unknown. From the very beginning, the game establishes itself as something different—horror not born from silence and shadows alone, but from overstimulation, disorientation, and the creeping realization that the celebration itself is hiding something deeply sinister.

The narrative is presented in an intentionally fragmented and mysterious way. You arrive at the so-called Headless Rave Festival with little context, guided by cryptic messages and vague instructions delivered through your in-game smartphone. As you explore, it becomes increasingly clear that the festival is more than a party; it is tied to an ancient, ritualistic presence that feeds on participation and attention. Storytelling unfolds subtly through environmental details, text messages, symbols, and conversations, leaving players to piece together meaning rather than spelling everything out directly. This approach reinforces the game’s dreamlike tone, making the plot feel more like a disturbing urban legend than a traditional horror narrative.

Gameplay focuses almost entirely on exploration and puzzle-solving rather than combat. There are no weapons or direct means of defense, which shifts the tension toward awareness and navigation. The smartphone plays a central role, acting as both a narrative device and a gameplay tool. By tracking Wi-Fi signals scattered throughout the festival grounds, players are guided toward points of interest, puzzles, and story progression. This mechanic gives the experience a scavenger-hunt feel, encouraging curiosity while also creating unease as signals draw you deeper into unfamiliar and increasingly disturbing areas. Occasional threats and hostile encounters force you to rely on movement and quick thinking rather than confrontation, heightening vulnerability.

Visually, Strobophagia is one of its strongest offerings. The game embraces a psychedelic aesthetic filled with glowing colors, exaggerated costumes, towering statues, and dynamic lighting that constantly shifts the mood. The rave environment is dense with visual stimuli, creating a sense of sensory overload that mirrors the themes of excess and loss of control. This visual intensity makes the horror feel surreal rather than grounded, blurring the line between reality and hallucination. The contrast between lively dance areas and darker, more ominous forest paths sustains tension, as players are never quite sure where danger may emerge.

Sound design and music play a crucial role in shaping the experience, though their effectiveness can vary. Electronic and rave-inspired tracks fit the setting and help establish immersion, especially during moments of exploration among crowds. However, the audio landscape sometimes struggles to maintain consistent intensity, with certain sections feeling quieter or less impactful than expected. While the music reinforces the theme, it does not always fully capitalize on opportunities to amplify fear or tension, occasionally leaving moments that feel emotionally underpowered despite strong visuals.

The pacing of the game is relatively short and focused, with a single playthrough typically lasting only a few hours. This brevity makes the experience feel more like a concentrated horror vignette than a full-length adventure. Multiple endings and hidden narrative elements offer some replay value, particularly for players interested in uncovering every detail or exploring alternate outcomes. Still, the compact structure means that Strobophagia leaves a stronger impression through atmosphere and concept than through mechanical depth or long-term engagement.

Technical execution is functional but not flawless. While the game generally runs as intended, minor bugs, unclear objectives, and occasional navigation confusion can interrupt immersion. These rough edges are noticeable, especially in a title that relies heavily on atmosphere and player engagement. That said, the artistic ambition often compensates for these shortcomings, and many players are willing to forgive technical imperfections in exchange for such a distinctive experience.

Ultimately, Strobophagia | Rave Horror is best understood as an experimental blend of horror, art installation, and interactive narrative. It does not rely on jump scares or traditional survival mechanics, instead cultivating dread through symbolism, overstimulation, and uncertainty. Its strengths lie in its visual identity, thematic originality, and willingness to explore horror through an unconventional lens. While its short length, uneven audio, and limited mechanical complexity may prevent it from appealing to everyone, it stands out as a memorable and daring entry in the indie horror space. For players seeking a horror experience that feels strange, hypnotic, and unlike anything else on Steam, Strobophagia offers a haunting journey into a rave where the music never truly stops—and neither does the sense that something is terribly wrong.

Next we need a VR game with this, lets make a new genre, get crazy with it!", "LOVE the first half of this game. The rave horror theme, music and cellphone mechanics are incredible.
