Holy Cow, wrong game!", 'Just finished the game; it's ok.

The best about it is definitely the visuals, which aren't incredible but pretty good to this day. Combined with the surprisingly really good audio too and it's a somewhat immersive experience which is very impressive considering how simple the game looks.

The two main complaints are with the gameplay and with the environmental storytelling, but let's start with gameplay since that's the bigger issue here.

While the platforming is actually very well-tuned, the puzzles definitely have a "trial and error" style of working, are very simple, or becoming timing-based later on. The best ones are definitely the timing-based puzzles which are pretty good but where it starts to get annoying is when the puzzle is already obvious very early on or it becomes obvious after dying a couple of times and what it basically amounts to is the majority of actual gameplay being slowly walking from point A to point B, and it's especially frustrating when you make a small mistake from being killed by something near the very end of a puzzle that was extremely hard to see coming, and then you have to repeat everything all over again which is just boring because of the really slow movement (and this happens way more than it should). Basic gameplay is fine on its own, but it has to be fun in some way (like just being faster, for example), otherwise it just feels like padding. And that's what this game falls into.

Secondly is the story. Environmental storytelling can be done very excellently, but here it's only decently done. At the end of the day it's a balancing act of exposition and interpretation when you do this kind of storytelling, but this game leans way too far into interpretation most of the time (there are a couple of exceptions, but most of the time) for it to feel genuinely meaningful by the end. So it's ok overall.

I'd really only recommend this for people who are really into the style or games that go extremely far into environmental storytelling (too far, in this particular case), because there are short games out there that are actually a blast to play or just generally much more meaningful (the first Portal and Before Your Eyes come to mind; both amazing experiences). All said, this gets a 5.5/10 (so C-tier).', "Loved the mobile version, loved the Steam version. Short puzzlegame with extremely minimalistic graphics and sound design, and no explicit storytelling, but good puzzle variety. You will need to decide for yourself whether this niche is for you, if the screenshots and videos on the storepage don't make you want to play it, you probably won't enjoy it. Debatably worth it at full price and definitely when discounted. Big recommend.", "the game itself isn't bad, but it freezes at the very end for some reason, and the game while is running I must press pause and unpause just to make it move 1 frame at a time....", 'Bought this game over a decade ago, and finally got around to playing it.
Delightful, beautiful, and fun.
', "Alright, don't have much to say cause the game is actually FIRE!. the things that makes me so frustrating is some of the challenges are so hard and takes many attemps and I mean a LOT OF ATTEMPS. But the Atmosphere and the gameplay is easy. the challenges are very creative but I would say there's not clearer narrative and storyline. like at the end, I guess we meet our Girlfriend maybe. I don't know because most of the challenges are to get out of the forest but Overall, a solid good game.", 'Is good :]', 'Probably one of those games that’s been in my backlog since the dawn of time.', "I hate this puzzle

I don't wanna play this game again", 'FUN!', 'LIMBO is a fantastic puzzle platformer(with slightly disappointing 2nd half), that weirdly reminds me a bit of early internet trolling/hard games, but in a good way

What i mean is that, the gameplay is designed around danger lurking everywhere, one misstep, one wrong move, one blink, and you're dead. But what LIMBO does differently, is taking said gameplay and making it way more fair. Cheap deaths rarely if ever happen, almost all of them are either telegraphed, obvious or just required you to pay attention to your surroundings, or to solve physics puzzle. It also has "gory deaths" aspect of those old flash games, but due to the artstyle, they arent always noticeable, and feel less like cheap shock horror, and more like interesting art direction

But LIMBO isn't just a nostalgic reminder of the old internet games, it does have a very creative, hard to describe puzzle types: 1) Physics based, where you have to figure out what to do 2) platforming, which sometimes asks you to be VERY precise. First type feels very rewarding, in same way Portal series feels satisfying to solve, and you never feel like solutions are dumb or hard to figure out, and the second type is satisfying in same way something like Super Meat Boy is but obviously way way easier n less common then insuper meat boy. 

There's also subtype of the second one, which I'd call chases, its especially prominent in the first half of the game, where you basically chased by something, and have to figure what to do fast. Encounters with that spider alone make this such a memorable experience. The way he relentlessly chases you, the way you fight back against him(and other living enemies), its all so, LIMBO, and I dont think I ever encountered anything like it.

Then the second half of the game happens, its not bad by any means, its still mostly good, but it just feels way less unique, more formulaic. The puzzles are still all physics based, but they have a lot of repeats, like "the section where you press/pull something n you have to leave area fast before dying" or "press/pull this thing a few times", Platforming is mostly same, but now there's less urgent moments, less chases, Speaking of chases, while we do have some sort of chases in 2nd half of the game, you arent running from monsters anymore, just from some sawblades etc. Which sucks, because, imho it ruins the atmosphere. Forest area in the beginning is insanely memorable not just because of fantastic art direction, or sound design but also because the gameplay makes it feel like a proper hostile forest. You genuinely feel alone, lost, even almost afraid while exploring it, but inside the factory onward, its just kinda... there... I hoped that at any moment spider may come back(unlikely but maybe he had a family), or a new threat will come to terrorise us, but it never happened. It felt like 2nd half of the game was made by different devs.

Even storytelling, which wasnt too prominent in the forest area, gets even less relevant in 2nd half. Forest at least had this mystery of when you find first living people, it makes your mind think "who are they, why are they here, why are they hostile to me?", its a strong hook, that made me want to know more... 2nd half of the game drops any apperances of them, you just see your sister twice, get no explanation of anything n thats it.

Despite my criticism at the end, I still HIGHLY recommend this game to basically anyone who hasnt played it yet, even if you hate puzzle genre on principle, LIMBO is different enough that you might still enjoy it(plus its hardest achievement is fun to hunt). I just, really wish second half of the game was as exciting, and memorable as the forest which I'll never forget.', 'As vezes você se sente um gênio, as vezes você sente um merda', 'what a game man w sound design i cant believe its just black and white but it was fun every puzzle makes me think .it was on too the point nice game enjoyed', 'ვაიმეეე', 'definitely not my type of game i wish i could refund', 'Great game, Looks Good on an OLED', 'arguably the most iconic platformer of the 2010s so yes I would recomeend']

['the original', 'I NEED A MED BAG!', "This may be the most terrifying shooter I've played, only because this isn't Payday 2", "Very dated game now, only grab it on a steep steep sale, if you want to see what the original Payday was like. The sequel Payday 2 is the best in the series.
For example, I got this for like £0.80 to sample what the series would be like before making a decision to buy Payday 2 on it's launch - and still have no desire to return 13 years later.", 'Eu adoro jogos que fazem apologia ao crime.', 'Payday the heist is a love story about four men who perpetually ♥♥♥♥ up in getting info somehow, thanks to bain', '10/10', "This is a game that has nothing much to show. It's entirely dependent on people making their own fun in multiplayer rather than the game itself being fun. It's just monotonous with the same enemies, crappy dialogue and boring guns. Potentially a bit fun if you care to play with others, but it'd be nice if the heists were at least fun enough to play once each on your own, but that'd mean good design and fun mechanics. I don't really like when games are made good only because they make for a silly time with others.", 'BEST. CREW. EVERRRRRRRR.', '.', 'really good game', "Despite being heavily overshadowed by its more populair younger brother, Payday The Heist still holds up great! 

Just like comparing L4D1 to L4D2, PD:TH has kept its original intended atmosphere and hasn't been muddied by constant updates. This game feels and plays exactly as intended in its original vision.

The game feels slightly less fluid than PD2, but the gameplay is still classic Payday. This game is also a lot tougher despite the thinner hordes of cops. Each individual swat officer is dangerous and can put you in the ground if you're not careful. All swat can wield shotguns, SMGs or ARs, making the normal 'horde' a lot more varied. Waves are shorter in general and there is more breathing room between waves, making the assaults feel more natural and impactful. 

Each weapon hits hard, including your own arsenal. Shattering the glass visor of heavy swats will never not be satisfying, and the genuine threat each special is when facing them compared to their PD2 counterparts where a bulldozer is as common as the amount of early retirements.

You start out very weak, and even Normal difficulty will start as a challenge. You really feel each difficulty jump and with the lack of healing methods each bit of HP is precious to try and preserve.

Of course with only 9 heists repetition is bound to set in. The weapon roster is also on the meager side. The Wolfpack DLC is absolutely required to crank both of these up and is downright essential to playing PD:TH.

It may be forgotten by most, and all of its contents may have been ported over to the sequel, but PD:TH still stands the test of time and differentiates itself with the challenge, tone, style, and simplicity that you cannot find in the gaming scene these days. It is absolutely worth checking out if you enjoy the sequel.", "for heist game and fps it's so good i love this game<3333
", 'Best Payday game better than Payday 3 in every possible way it has a soul in it', 'Idioti movements of bots. 
No possibility to go full stealth. 
Felt like playing Left 4 Dead :D 
Never recommending this s*** to anyone.', "What this game lacks in content and customization it makes up for in atmosphere and genuinely challenging and engaging heists. As fun of a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ payday 2 is, it's exactly that, a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, Payday: The Heist feels exactly like what it was designed to be, a fun, exciting heist game that feels straight out of the movies. 8/10", 'money all mine

The gameplay loops is less linear then Payday 2 and the difficulty in this game is insane. If you're not good at playing on Overkill on Payday 2 then you might need to practice before playing on Normol on this game. Honestly goated.", 'PD:TH > Payday 3', 'this is some good ♥♥♥♥ bro like damn, love being apart of the big payday gang', "buy it if you're corious, but it's like playing an old, way harder payday2 demo", 'EGPYTIAN POOR CAT Will understand when he is older.......', 'игра грабители, плохо', "Payday: The Heist is fifteen years old this year, and it's still possibly one of the greatest horde shooters of all time. That includes some fierce modern competition: Helldivers, Killing Floor, and even its own sequels. None can quite beat the first Payday.

The gunplay is smooth and the heists are perfect. Every weapon handles well (they better, since there are so few), and the four upgrade trees provide clear goals and make every weapon (and its upgrades) feel like a reward. Cops die when you hit them in the head and are not bullet sponges, but neither are you: You are incredibly vulnerable, especially at higher levels, and one mistake or tactical error can cost you an entire heist (and wonderfully, make it feel like your fault, not the game's). You die in seconds, but so do the enemies. At higher difficulties, it almost becomes a strategy game. The gameplay loop is without error: Assault, cooldown, and assault again, and between these things try to do an objective, but the originality and flair of each heist never makes it boring.

It eats the lunch of the movie Heat: Every heist has a punctual story, growing in volume until it comes to a bombastic finish (usually involving a large explosion or a helicopter). The characters are obnoxious, but not annoying. Apart from personal taste, it's almost impossible to hate any single character. Bain is a perfect announcer, and all four heisters are every movie archetype (The cool leader, the aggressive-but-cautious brawler, the suave cockney bruiser, and the downright insane pyromaniac). Their voice acting is personable and their personalities are worked into the heist through dialogue and conversations, making the game feel more like an interactive movie than a mindless shoot-em-up.

The story seems unimportant, but in combination with all other things perfects the game. No Payday game has actually managed to reach the heights of Payday 1's story. It is beautifully simple: It is the movie Heat, Point Break, and the bank heist scene from The Dark Knight, with all of the aesthetics and flair and none of the fat of a complicated story. The small selection of heists and the complexity of each creates a linear 'crime spree', with each heist growing more bombastic and ludicrous over time.

This all combines to create an experience that is completely unique. No other game, even its sequels, is like it. It does not feel like you are playing a game, but living out your own heist movie. It is creative, it is funny, it is simple, and it never gets boring. Get this game if you know what's good for you.", "PAYDAY™ The Heist has its advantages: for example, the last stealth mission is really good and is more memorable than the rest of the content. Plus, it's a pleasant surprise that the game works well on Linux without any extra problems or dancing around the bush.

However, unfortunately, this is not enough to make the game stand out. Most of the missions quickly become monotonous, the gunfights are outdated, the AI often behaves strangely, and the overall gameplay feels too raw and primitive today. The co-op mode can save the game a little, but overall, it lacks depth, variety, and usability", 'cool soundtrack', 'this game had very good animations of npcs, nice and smooth objectives but the game looks like if left 4 dead 2 and payday 2 was combined, also the reloading animation is not very smooth and not very easy for the average payday 2 player', "This was fun to play with friends back in the day, don't have a ton of time in it but from what I do remember it was a solid experience."]

name: SCARLET NEXUS
categories: ['Single-player', 'Steam Achievements', 'Full controller support', 'Steam Cloud', 'Family Sharing']
short_description: Choose between Yuito and Kasane, elite psionics each armed with a talent in psychokinesis and their own reason to fight. Complete both of their stories to unlock all the mysteries of a Brain Punk future caught between technology and psychic abilities.
detailed_description_raw:
Digital Deluxe Edition


The Deluxe Edition includes the SCARLET NEXUS full game and Brain Punk Bundle, along with 2 bonuses.


Ultimate Edition


The Ultimate Edition includes the full game, Season Pass, Digital Artbook & Soundtrack, an Attire Set, an Additional Attachment, and Plug-ins.


About the Game



In the far distant future, a psionic hormone was discovered in the human brain, granting people extra-sensory powers and changed the world as we knew it. As humanity entered this new era, deranged mutants known as Others began to descend from the sky with a hunger for human brains. Highly resistant to conventional attack methods, extreme measures needed to be taken to battle the overwhelming threat and preserve humanity. Those with acute extra-sensory abilities, known as psionics, were our only chance to fight the onslaught from above. Since then, psionics have been scouted for their talents and recruited to the Other Suppression Force (OSF), humanity’s last line of defense.



Featuring a dual story, begin your adventure with either Yuito Sumeragi, an energetic recruit from a prestigious political family or Kasane Randall, the mysterious scout whose power and skill has gained great notoriety among the OSF. As their different experiences interweave with each other, it is only then that you will reveal the full story and unlock all the mysteries of a Brain Punk future caught between technology and psychic abilities in SCARLET NEXUS.



Kinetic Psychic Combat – Using psycho-kinetic abilities, the world around you becomes your greatest weapon. Lift, break and throw pieces of your environment to build your attack combos and lay waste to your enemies.

Exterminate the Others – Deranged mutants that descended from the sky, highly resistant to conventional attack methods and defenses. Tormented by the constant pain of their mutation, they seek brains of living organisms to calm their madness.

Discover a Brain Punk future – Explore and protect a futuristic Japanese landscape that combines inspirations from classic anime and western science fiction.

A Dual Story Experience – Dive into a complex story of bonds, courage and heroism, crafted by minds behind the iconic Tales of Vesperia.

reviews:
['I recommend this game.', "Honestly kind of boring. Combat is mediocre and the story isn't better than your average airing anime.", 'Recommend if on sale.

Combat is fun for a while, but can get tiresome after your 2nd playthrough. Most enemies are sponges. In my experience you pretty much have to combo to stun to use your finisher to kill things in a respectable amount of time. EX GAME (think new game + but from the other char's perspective) doesn't scale, so you end up being lvl 60 ish killing lvl 10 stuff, makes replayabilty a cakewalk which isn't necessarily a bad thing.

Story is straight up garbage. Stopped caring halfway through first playthrough. If you actually want to follow the story, don't start as the male (Yuito). I did and it just made no sense. The female (Kasane) offers more explanation as to the events of the plot and the "why"... which Yuito's story completely lacks.

It's just an "ok" game. I enjoyed probably 28-30 hrs of the 37 hrs I put into the game, and only really did 37 to push for all the achievements.', 'Game plays well the combat system is good, story fails where it matters and will bring you sadness', "(game incomplete at 5 hours of gameplay at time of review, part of my review all my games effort)
(short version)
I just can't anymore... 5 hours of gameplay, constant new tutorial messages I feel like I'm studying for a college exam with everything that's being thrown at me.  I'm not simplistic I play complex games, but I despise it when a game has an 8 hour tutorial mode before the game actually starts.  You can do better than this Bandai.  $60 is way too much for what you get. I can see why it's frequently on an 84% sale.", 'takes a while to get going, mechanically and narratively, but if you got 10 bucks, 30 hours and a playstation vita where your heart should be, you should probably pick this up
', 'game bagus, grinding pun seru juga', 'i love scarlet nexus for everything u see. the whole story maybe bad but for me it was cool u lose friends then in the end u unit together and take out the final boss 3 phase by the way. the op 10/10 as for game play combat/gameplay 9/10 it could be better', 'I got it a discount and I thought that I may enjoy it despite reviews but sadly no
combat is just button mashing whatever
story I dont know since I was turned off by anime style characters
I also do not like the graphics or the city where I got to play around

I guess you have to like anime to play this', 'Pros:
- has a good story concept
- the characters are diverse and complex in their own way
- this game quite surprises me with plot twists

Cons:
- the combat is stupidly clunky (mostly in the earlier parts of the game, due to not progressing to the "skill tree". It is awfully tedious as well, a quarter chunk of it is defined by "finisher cutscenes", which the cutscenes in general are barely impressive to watch at it.
- The storytelling format is blatantly horrible. For a mature concept, the story telling has been extensively dumbed down, to the point I have to wonder who the target demographic did the developers made it for. There is too much hand-holding, as every character most of the time speak either bunch of fluff or repeat what has already been covered that could be avoided spoken entirely. In fact, cutting off half of the dialogue would not even affect the gameplay. It is drastically worse at the first two hours, you know... the refund window, so there was a slight chance I would have completely refunded the game with how bad the storytelling is.
- For an RPG game, the progression is too linear. Half of the game mechanics like equipment and level might as well not exist, because it doesn't even do much to the gameplay. I have no trouble fighting enemies that are 15 levels higher than me.

I always have a terrible experience when it comes to playing games published by Bandai Namco. And this one is no exception. I had to refund God Eater 3, and .hack//G.U Last Recode because the combat sucks and the story is worst at hooking during the first two hours of the gameplay. Yet I have to give this a chance, and played this until completion. I will never pick a game from Bandai Namco again, especially since I loved the Tekken Franchise', "Picked up. this game coz I craved some fighting gameplay  similar to Nier Automata, don't really care of the story skipped every cut-scene, gameplay is solid enough no way near as good as NA but still fun", 'The story is very engaging and dark; the combat is flawless, and the use of psychic powers is a cool concept. The dialogue can be a bit dry sometimes, but overall, I would definitely recommend it to others.', 'Satisfying mechanics, tolerable story, and not-horrible characters with development corresponding to maturity level. I like this game, and I wish I could see a sequel. If you want a game more souls-like, go for the hardest difficulty level, but if you want to progress, the second to hardest difficulty level is pretty satisfying.', 'Really fun anime action RPG. The combat can get a bit repetitive after a while and the characters like to talk just a bit too much but overall its a solid game with very satisfying combat.', "This game... I don't know how to explain it but I played it for 3 straight days.... it made me feel like the world blew my emotional state and then after I beat it... THERE IS MORE?! I wouldn't normally say this but my emotional damage from this game has sent me through the moon. it plays out perfectly from day one and even though it feels a little jank at first with the combat not being fluid once you level up the game makes you lose grip on reality. Then the relationship system in the game makes you feel for all the characters and you sit there fighting back the world like its the real deal... I don't know how to process after this... Thank you Developers for your seriously amazing experience... if you want a game tester... please... I would be all in... no hesitation. heart goes out to you and this entire work. I know its been a long while since its been out but I bought it a while ago... can't even pretend this didn't make my backlist because I didn't realize how good it would be.", "Anime tropes the video game. And that's why I love it. If there's more of these games like Astral Chain and Scarlet Nexus, send me tips pls :)", 'Excellent game. 

Very anime in a very good way.
Pretty good gameplay depth with different ways of playing.
Basically(?) completely voiced, which is ultra-rare, across lots of characters.', 'The game starts good
But repetitive after 7 hours the gameplay is not bad  the game should be 12 hours', "Awsome game, awsome fights, awsome sound, awsome story, awsome graphics, awsome everything !
Honestly it's an awsome game and worth it a lot !", 'The combat in this game is fun, and somewhat challenging.

However, the writing is atrocious. There's bad exposition, and then there's this game. It's CSI-level bad, exposition wise. The characters tell each other things that would--in reality--be basic knowledge to anyone in their world, from talking about the founders of their society (and why a particular shrine exists for who should be the most important person in the society's history) to talking about why these characters have their powers. You might counter that maybe the characters are just that dumb, but seemingly everyone comes from a family of prominence, which should mean the best education money can buy. Oh, and they put a sidequest in the game meant to get you to watch their anime, and the "story" that comes out of it is so cringe and laughably stupid... That's about how far I am into the game. It says I've played for around 11 hours, but its more like 5 or 6 hours with me going afk for the rest of the time.

Also, the grinding looks like it will become tedious for if and when you decided to upgrade weapons (you're required to farm drops from mobs, which will probably require you to find the specific mobs you are farming from in a scenario, clear them, hope they drop what you need, and then restart the scenario and repeat).

Another annoyance, especially for a game they are charging like $80 for is that the majority of the cutscenes aren't animated. They're still frames with an additional frame on either side to show the characters in conversation and you watch their mouths move kind-of in sync with the speaking. (The main dialogue is fully voiced)

Definitely, buy it on sale if you like action-rpg combat and don't care about inane dialogue that starts with a purpose and then gets interrupted so that said purpose is either never addressed or so that proverbial "can" can be perpetually kicked down the road. Otherwise, skip.', "Buy it on sale. It's good but not great. Combat is the main appeal. The story and the characters are decent, but the moment-to-moment dialogue is very robotic. Performance was flawless for me.", 'I didn't hate my time with this game. Story is about 25ish hours long, and it was admittedly the wakest part of the adventure. Combat is fun and good paced with the brain stuff being actually fun to use. There's this "standby phase" between story chapters where you interact with you team similar to Persona which wasn't that bad to muck with.

Ultimately on sale its a great time waster if you're looking for fun combat and don't expect award winning story.', 'I still pray for the release of a Scarlet Nexus 2.', 'really fun game, i like the world and the combat, and oh my god i LOVE the soundtrack. my only gripe about this whole game is that the enemies are called "others" like damn, could you not think of another name?', 'worth getting on mega sale, like 50-60% off or higher. story is riddled with problems, but the characters are alright, and the main combat is good. worldbuilding is neat, i wanna copy the psychic power system into other games.', 'Good game and best when played twice with both the leads and their routes. I have just finsihed the first route and now started the second. The combat is good if you like action games.', 'I cannot recommend Scarlet Nexus, and it’s not because of gameplay or story — it’s because the game literally breaks and prevents you from progressing.

In Chapter 5 (Kasane route), I encountered a game-breaking bug where after defeating all enemies, the barrier wouldn’t disappear and the game still thinks you’re “in battle.” There are no enemies left, no indicators, nothing — just a soft lock that completely stops your progress.

And the worst part? This is not a rare issue.

Multiple players report the exact same bug: killing all enemies but the barrier “doesn’t go down”

Some players even had to restart entire sections or playthroughs because nothing fixed it

One workaround people suggested was literally using cheats to jump over the barrier — which says everything about how broken this is

I tried:

Reloading saves

Restarting the mission

Searching for hidden enemies

Nothing worked.

This isn’t just a minor glitch — it’s a progression-blocking bug in the main story, and for a story-driven game, that’s unacceptable.

It’s especially frustrating because the game actually has good ideas — stylish combat and interesting mechanics — but none of that matters when the game can randomly lock you out of continuing.', 'I was so happy when Yutio went "I'm Yuito" and then Yuitoed all over the place', 'animu slop repacked as a lazy videogame cash grab
(I didn't know the anime scarlet nexus existed while I was playing)

kindergarden level dialogue

bad localization
(tooltip for unequipping is called "Disengage." it's also the only tooltip that ends with a period)

annoying ui controls (have to select your 1 character separately to change gear)

map design is ass (jaywalk prevention(?????) that forces you to run to the nearest staircase to cross the street)

nothing here is worth 82% off the full price, and definitely nothing worth 70 bucks
the character that acts as a save point recommends you "patronize" his services, perhaps he means you should  patronize this game with your wallet.', 'Hidden gem']

name: MechWarrior Online™ Legends
categories: ['Multi-player', 'MMO', 'Steam Achievements', 'In-App Purchases']
short_description: MechWarrior Online™ Legends is a tactical, 'Mech-based online shooter set in the vast BattleTech Universe. Assume the role of an elite MechWarrior, piloting powerful BattleMechs, fighting for supremacy over the Inner Sphere.
detailed_description_raw:
MechWarrior Online™ Legends is a tactical, 'Mech-based online shooter set in the vast BattleTech Universe.

As a Rookie MechWarrior, you will have immediate access to 16 pre-configured powerful mechanized units called BattleMechs. As you earn in-game currency and experience you will be able to purchase and customize your own stable of awesome Battlemechs. Monthly events will grant you access to even more items, currency, and 'Mechs to help you progress your MechWarrior career. Find your perfect Mech, create your perfect loadout, unlock all your 'Mech Skills, and customize your 'Mech inside and out!

MechWarrior Online™ Legends Quickplay, Faction Play, Private Match, and an Event Queue with custom modes!

Quickplay - Quick Play is where you'll find fast action, fielding your 'Mech in team-based game modes and maps. Pick your Favorite Mech and Drop in Quickplay for intense 'Mech Battles featuring 10+ maps and 4 + modes to keep 

Faction Play - In Faction Play you will field 4 ‘Mechs per match, lending your skills and allegiance to the Faction of your choosing in prolonged team-based engagements, conquering and defending planets in their name to earn rewards and expand your Faction's territory across the Inner Sphere.

Event Queue - Be on the lookout for special events such as the monthly Solaris weekend with 1 vs. 1 and 2 vs. 2 intense fights or bring up to 4 mechs in Drop Deck modes to keep you in the fight longer. Sometimes things really get crazy with Unlimited Ammo and Extra Armor modifiers so keep an eye out for the Event Queue.

Become a Legend in MechWarrior Online™ Legends.

['coolio!', "Old school mech combat that is still going strong. As good as it's ever been, and can be played casually.", 'Great franchise', 'The LRMs move from the progenitor in a slanted manner...', '12 Vs 12 MECH FIGHTING!?!?!?', 'best and worst game ever. edging towards the best', "my goodness. This game is fun. After many years of playing, I still come back to this one. It's a niche game and player-base but man are the customizations fun. Will keep playing till.....well maybe i die...", 'HELP ME I AM ADDICTED TO THIS GAME', 'Its a Good Mech Game And Free, if its not your vibe uninstall ftw
', 'Is good game, i like yes', 'Extraordinarily well balanced shooter, lots of options for different playstyles. Relatively friendly gamer community, the online players enjoy playing with others. Satisfies that stomp -stomp for sure!!! 5 stars from me!', 'Great game.     Except for training entirely player verses player keeping it fresh.  All kinds of customizilaby of units, and instead of pay to to win, you pay to keep it fresh.  You can also win through playing all kinds of free in game items and equipment.', 'Mechs yes', 'This is the greatest online mech game EVER its like those old mobile games but actually good!', 'funny game i can only get assists
', 'GOIDA GAME but matchmaking should be BV based. Fighting 5 Kodiaks is not fun. Other than matchmaking game is good.', 'its hard to log in', '7/10 would start succession war again', "This game is pay to win. I found that it didn't ruin my experience, but it's still a factor to consider. With that said, you can do way worse than MWO.

I can't wholeheartedly recommend a P2W game, but this is one of the least egregious. I'd choose this over WoT or WT any day, especially with how awesome the community usually is.

Unfortunately, PGI was subjected to some pretty extreme layoffs about a week ago. Worst of all, they laid off Alex Iglesias, the man behind just about every modern mech design. I don't think the franchise is dead, but we're certainly closer to that point now than we were a few weeks ago.", "Old, but extremely fun. It is more than worth one's time.
", "So far this seem pretty awesome. Very complex and lots of details, many I'm sure I'll never learn, but it's already fun so I'll stick around for more.", 'Fantastic game!', 'Great game, I highy', "This game is a blast. I wish matches styles were more varied but it's all about the mechs.", "PGI's original game... a f2p pvp mech-sim... it's good! Has a steep learning curve but is very fun to play, solo or with friends.", "Easy to play and you usually don't die immediately.", "Only play this if you like battletech

Its not a perfect game by any means, and if could certainly use a few features.
(melee, 8v8's, etc)

But I haven't payed a cent and I'm happily armed and get to play around with interesting builds", 'MWO is a bad game that could be a really good game if given at least a bit of love (which it will never get, since it is very much in a self-induced state of clinical death). 

tl;dr play it only if you are hopelessly in love with the setting, don't waste your time otherwise. But I don't need to tell you, you are already one of us cursed people here anyway. 

There are multiple layers to this. 

1) MWO might be the most basic, shallow interpretation of Battletech/Mechwarrior. There are only mechs (no other vehicles), there is no melee/no physical damage, the weapon roster is limited, and the movement is very basic, too. There is no environmental damage model, either. 
For all intents and purposes, there is exactly one (1) game mode you can actually reliably (!) get matches in, and that's the Quick Play (public play). Which, in turn, is, for all intents and purposes, a team deathmatch. 

2) Even though it has been on the market for more than a decade, the game's technical state is simply bad. The netcode is, without a doubt, garbage. Rubberbanding, lagging, and, worst of all, the hit register are tragic. You can fairly often see your projectiles going through the enemy mech's model without causing any damage. Sometimes, the damage doesn't register at all, even with visual cues. Sometimes, damage registers in the back armor, even though the hit was scored from the front. You can mess up the hitreg even further by going fast enough, by abusing jump jets (so widespread is this issue that JJ fluttering is considered a must-learn technique for light mech pilots), and much more. 

3) Maps. MWO maps range from middling to a point where you could use some of them to teach how to NOT make a multiplayer map. All of them, however, enforce certain ways of playing, even further dumbing down any potential tactics the genre and the setting could potentially offer. This is also tied to point 2), because nearly all of the maps are STILL full of points where you can get stuck, LOD issues, floating objects, flickering textures, etc. Furthermore, in most cases (there are some exceptions), the map designers seemingly had no idea how to work with the mech scale. As a result, most of the maps play as regular human-sized FPS maps, with props simply resized to more or less "correct" scale. This applies to everything. This translates into ground textures that have "pebbles" the size of a car and "bushes" that work like grass for mechs that are the size of a small house. This means that most (although there are exceptions) maps completely kill any sense of being in a big stompy mech the moment you start noticing the environment and not just the enemy.
Finally, the game allows you to use customized .cfg files to remove foliage, water, etc., with all the implications this brings. 

4) Matchmaking
The matchmaking in Quick Play (public play) is a joke, simple as. I've seen another review claiming it is the worst in gaming. I think this might not be a hyperbole. Not only are you allowed to drop with the whole lance of friends/clan members(!), which immediately throws any attempt at balancing the teams out of the window (no matter the small playerbase, this shows the developer's fundamental misunderstanding of what public play means); worse yet, the system as a whole is simply not functional. Matches commonly result in 12:0/1/2/3 roflstomps, the issue only further exacerbated by the same-clan friends dropping together or by streamers doing the same (to get those juicy kill videos). It is not uncommon to have up to 8+ matches long losing/winning streaks. This is a self-replicating issue, too: a small player count means difficulty getting matches > which means beginners are thrown against vets > which results in uneven roflstomps that are not fun for anyone > which results in fewer (new) players. And the cycle continues. If you play the game long enough, you can fairly accurately predict if the match will be even or not by simply looking at players' names, thus doing a better job than the matchmaking "system." Finally, the time to get a match has been increasing lately; waiting ~5 minutes (for a 4-minute roflstomp, again) is not unheard of. 

5) Balancing. While I understand that truly balancing a game like Mechwarrior might be an impossible task, the way MWO does it is, again, both lazy and inapt (which are two trademarks for PGI). It was an issue when it was purely PGI, and it is an issue now, when Cauldron (community) does it. This is done either in the form of the typical glass house neverending juggling with marginal 5% buff/nerf here and there, which does absolutely nothing, or worse, by constantly pushing certain ways of play (or mechs) favored by various members of the Cauldron, or by power creep established by Legendary (paid) mechs (and their subsequent nerfing when everyone starts playing with these), the game balance is a mess. 

6) Visuals. While obviously dated, the visuals still have to make one wonder how the hell PGI actually managed to make CryEngine 3 look this bad. This especially applies to some of the newer maps, like Luthien. There are Quake 1-2 mods that look (and run) better than this. Again, there are exceptions. The optimization ranges (again) from pretty neat, to sillybad. 

7) Sound. The sound is okay, although quality ranges from pretty great (ballistics, generally) to just silly (some lasers), but there's a reason why the most popular mods on Nexus are soundpacks. Music is the usual mechmetal slop that generally serves as a good reminder to turn off the music. I yearn for the days of the Stutter Shark instead of the Generic Metal Solo #59514(TM)

There is more trouble, like abhorrent UI, incorrectly set VFX effects that will burn your eyeballs, (fixable with custom cfg, but still), more balancing problems, shady business/P2W practices, and more. I could spend hours writing these down, but the review is already enough of a ramble as it is. Let this thing die, and let someone else take the reins of Mechwarrior from PGI.', "Pay to win game with a severely toxic userbase.

Not worth playing, if you're looking for the real MechWarrior experience play the old games or MW5 Mercinaries", 'Best online PvP MechWarrior game out there. Been playing since MW2']

name: 鬼谷八荒 Tale of Immortal
categories: ['Single-player', 'Steam Achievements', 'Full controller support', 'Steam Workshop', 'Steam Cloud', 'Remote Play on Tablet', 'Family Sharing']
short_description: Tale of Immortal is an open-world sandbox based on Chinese mythology and cultivation. You will grow to become immortal, conquer the beasts from the Classic of Mountains and Season, make your choices carefully and grasp your own destiny.
detailed_description_raw:
Premium Edition


About the Game

Tale of Immortal is an open-world sandbox game based on Chinese mythology and cultivation. You will grow to become immortal, conquer the beasts from the Classic of Mountains and Season, make your choices carefully and grasp your own destiny.



Step into the enchanting world of the Tale of Immortal, where cultivation is an art that requires more than just financial means and help from fellow cultivators. To truly master it, one must possess a deep understanding of the craft and have access to the right cultivation venues. But that's not all—the journey is fraught with danger, as you'll face unpredictable monsters, crisis-ridden mystic realms, insurmountable barriers, and brilliant treasures waiting to be discovered.

As a novice cultivator in the Qi Refining stage, you'll set out from Yukong Village to cultivate your inner strength, defy the odds, and overcome adversity. With each breakthrough, you'll be able to rewrite your destiny and cleave through the thorns on your journey. Cross continents, witness the wonders of the world, and then ascend to Tian Yuan Mountain, where the ultimate enlightenment of the Way awaits.



Embrace a fully-realized NPC world, where each character is a unique entity with their own friends, family, and life trajectory— completely independent of your progress. However, if you choose to meddle, you certainly can!

The game is yours to shape as you wish. Collaborate with your beloved to save the day, seek revenge alone, or even become the enemy of all. The choice is yours to make.

Master martial arts, teach NPCs the Taoist Mind, learn from seasoned gurus, and accept eager disciples. You can also take on the NPCs in the auction house and follow it up by drawing your sword or claiming your hard-earned spoils.

As part of a group of immortal cultivators, you can either shine bright or cultivate in secrecy. It's all up to you and the path you choose to take.



Are you ready to embark on a thrilling adventure? One where you might become the hero who saves the day and wins the heart of the love of your life? Or maybe you're looking to stir up some jealousy in your crush and have them fight for your affection? How about fulfilling a fifty-year-old promise or getting caught up in the struggle of a mysterious organization? Perhaps you'll uncover a long-forgotten treasure, only to have it snatched away by a cunning adversary?

The possibilities are endless!

But that's just the beginning! As you journey through this immersive world, apart from interacting with a rich cast of NPCs, you'll encounter a plethora of random events and side tasks that will keep you on your toes. Some might leave you feeling frustrated or hurt, while others could be just the thing to save you or make you stronger after facing danger head-on. Every decision you make will shape the narrative, leading to a unique and unforgettable story that's yours alone.



Do you want to take your sect to new heights of power and glory? We've got the ultimate sect management gameplay that lets you experience the full range of sect life: from humble disciple to mighty sect master. Join a massive sect and work your way up from the bottom, or take on the challenge of leading a fallen sect back to prominence.

As a Disciple, you'll complete sect missions, compete in sect tournaments, and fight for glory and resources for your sect.

As an Elder, you'll be responsible for teaching and guiding your disciples, assigning tasks, and accelerating the sect's growth.

The real power and responsibility, however, lie with the Sect Leader. You'll need to keep order in the sect, plan strategic attacks and defenses, and fight for precious resources like Spirit Mines. If you're ambitious enough, you can even conquer other sects and seize their exclusive "Rewrite Destiny" and manuals.

Achieve your wildest ambitions and unify the world, as long as you have the strength and the drive to make it happen.



To embark on an epic adventure, you need to hone your skills and become a true warrior.

Start by building a solid foundation of cultivation. Your mind skills are crucial to success, so focus on cultivating a powerful mindset. From there, you can delve into martial and spiritual skills to take your abilities to the next level. As you explore, keep an eye out for opportunities to create edges with your motion and special skills. And don't forget to tap into your ultimate skills to save the day when necessary.

There are a dozen of manuals to choose from, each with its own unique characteristics. Will you specialize in one of the twelve Spiritual Roots, or master them all? What school of martial arts will you establish? The choice is yours.

There are treasures to be found in every corner of this world. From the Manual Pavilion to the mystic realms and beyond, you never know what you might discover. And with hundreds of rare and exotic monsters, fellow cultivators, ghosts, demons, and immortals to test your strength against, you'll never be bored.

If you're up for a challenge, why not try your luck at the Immortal Tournament or the Master Rankings? Can you break through the barriers of the Nether Mountains? Will your school rise to the occasion and prove its worth? As you journey through this perilous world, your school will be put to the test.



Sharpen your skills and harness the power of tools!

At the start, you get to choose from three formidable weapons, each offering a distinct cultivation system. The Bagua Jade grants you the ability to revive yourself and your chosen ally, the Eye of Providence allows you to steal Beast Skills and gain unparalleled insights into the world, and the Mythical Gourd empowers you to capture a Boss and enlist it as your loyal sidekick: command it to fight by your side or to search for treasure on your behalf.

And that's not all. Refine your own Artifacts and summon Artifact Spirits to fight by your side. These Spirits not only offer you companionship but also trigger exclusive adventures and provide invaluable assistance.

Moreover, hone your skills in practicing Alchemy and Talismans, mastering the art of Geomancy and Feng Shui, and crafting a plethora of items to assist you in your explorations. Confuse your enemies, slay your foes, and emerge victorious every time!


Our studio hopes that in this game, players will express the theme of "Always stick to your heart, dare to fight against difficulties; make choices carefully and grasp your own destiny."

Join a massive sect and work your way up from the bottom, or take on the challenge of leading a fallen sect back to prominence. As a Disciple, you'll complete sect missions, compete in sect tournaments, and fight for glory and resources for your sect. As an Elder, you'll be responsible for teaching and guiding your disciples, assigning tasks, and accelerating the sect's growth. The real power and responsibility, however, lie with the Sect Leader. You'll need to keep order in the sect, plan strategic attacks and defenses, and fight for precious resources like Spirit Mines. If you're ambitious enough, you can even conquer other sects and seize their exclusive "Rewrite Destiny" and manuals. Achieve your wildest ambitions and unify the world, as long as you have the strength and the drive to make it happen. To embark on an epic adventure, you need to hone your skills and become a true warrior. Start by building a solid foundation of cultivation. Your mind skills are crucial to success, so focus on cultivating a powerful mindset. From there, you can delve into martial and spiritual skills to take your abilities to the next level. As you explore, keep an eye out for opportunities to create edges with your motion and special skills. And don't forget to tap into your ultimate skills to save the day when necessary. There are a dozen of manuals to choose from, each with its own unique characteristics. Will you specialize in one of the twelve Spiritual Roots, or master them all? What school of martial arts will you establish? The choice is yours. There are treasures to be found in every corner of this world. From the Manual Pavilion to the mystic realms and beyond, you never know what you might discover. And with hundreds of rare and exotic monsters, fellow cultivators, ghosts, demons, and immortals to test your strength against, you'll never be bored. If you're up for a challenge, why not try your luck at the Immortal Tournament or the Master Rankings? Can you break through the barriers of the Nether Mountains? Will your school rise to the occasion and prove its worth? As you journey through this perilous world, your school will be put to the test. Sharpen your skills and harness the power of tools! At the start, you get to choose from three formidable weapons, each offering a distinct cultivation system. The Bagua Jade grants you the ability to revive yourself and your chosen ally, the Eye of Providence allows you to steal Beast Skills and gain unparalleled insights into the world, and the Mythical Gourd empowers you to capture a Boss and enlist it as your loyal sidekick: command it to fight by your side or to search for treasure on your behalf. And that's not all. Refine your own Artifacts and summon Artifact Spirits to fight by your side. These Spirits not only offer you companionship but also trigger exclusive adventures and provide invaluable assistance. Moreover, hone your skills in practicing Alchemy and Talismans, mastering the art of Geomancy and Feng Shui, and crafting a plethora of items to assist you in your explorations. Confuse your enemies, slay your foes, and emerge victorious every time! Our studio hopes that in this game, players will express the theme of "Always stick to your heart, dare to fight against difficulties; make choices carefully and grasp your own destiny."

3. click on the "change system locale"
4. apply "the beta: use unicode utf"
5. restart PC', 'I really love playing this game. I even bought it 2 more times, one on my phone and one for my other account. All in all if you like reading novels and want to have interactions with random encounters, cool fighting systems, and a hard time, this is for you. I usually play on chaos mode and try to be realistic on how I play, like how would it be, if one were in a cultivation game and already had cheats? (you usually die in Fundation building to Qi Condensation building tzz)', 'funn game for 1 play true', 'Best cultivation game ever made', "To better progress cultivation and protection I hit up 7 9/10 cuties. Then my 7 partners killed each other until the one with highest cultivation survived, so I kept no other side chicks.

Two months later, my sworn brother killed my newly conferred main girl. I killed my sworn brother out of anger, then his master, also my sect master, tried to kill me. For revenge I married the sect master's daughter.

Of course, being angry, the Sect master killed my wife/her daughter as well.  

Never have I seen a game comprehend and translate the cultivators' state of mind so perfectly. Very wise, very wise.

Jokes aside, the game has a high replayability with a set-up of destinies(chosen traits that give certain buffs/debuffs) and different martial/spiritual skills depending on weapon and talent type of your choosing.  You can switch your preferred martial/spiritual arts mid-game even, though it is a bit costly and may take a little time. 
Story is fine, some parts are beautiful, most are unpolished, and though there seems to be some level of agency given to you many story-central quests do end up leading to the same destination. 

Overall the best Cultivation/Xianxia rpg I have ever played. Very recommended if you love flavor-text stories and a little theory-crafting over your build.", 'Addictive game. Always come back to it!', 'Fantastic is the only word I can use to describe this game. Personally, I can read Chinese so most of the mods are accessible to me, and they do not disappoint if you can find them. I also played Amazing Cultivation Simulator, but this is more of an RPG so it hits a totally different spot.', 'you know what? for Chinese by race I dont speak Chinese ohh the curse of not born in the mainland, I think I onlky have Chinese name but most of the family stop speaking chinese like 3 generation ago.

anyway great game just most mods is in chinese, the game also somewhat sound like machine translated, I understand Chinese market alone is big but.... Please invest in good English localization, you guys doing great for game like WUWA and Star rail why cant it be replicated here, I understand budged maybe problematic but for long time investment and return maybe thats necessary sacrifice.

anyway the game is cheap, playable somewhat deep and I reccomend it.', 'i love the cultivation aspect so much. another that i dislike is the limit of moves you can do before it moves on to the next month as the loading takes too long on low end pc', 'good gmae', 'good', 'Right off the bat you’re thrown into a huge world where you start as a newbie cultivator and are supposed to become some kind of immortal legend. it sounds simple in theory, but your first hour is basically you button‑mashing, dying, and wondering why that giant beast just vaporized you like you weren’t even there. this world is gorgeous and full of stuff to do — quests, NPC dramas, and choices everywhere — but holy moly does it feel like the game expects you to read a thousand scrolls of wisdom first. combat can be surprisingly tactical. if you just spam attacks like some action junkie, you will end up on the floor faster than you can say “cultivation.” you need to think about timing, your build, and sometimes how the heck to even approach enemies that look way stronger than you. there’s a lot of depth here, and once you start getting the hang of the systems it feels pretty rewarding. it’s not holding your hand, but that’s part of the charm — and the salt. the world is also massive. you can wander around, take side quests that feel like mini stories, manage your cultivation path, and even influence NPCs in ways you didn’t expect. big sandbox energy, and sometimes that also means “oh I spent 3 hours doing random stuff and forgot to save.” yep. that happened. it’s worth noting that player feedback on Steam is mixed overall — some people love the complexity and freedom, others find parts of it grindy or rough around the edges — so it’s not a universally adored masterpiece, but it definitely has a passionate community.

and of course, sometimes you’re like:
“ok I finally understand this system”
…then ten mechanics later you’re back to being confused again. classic.

end of the day you’ll:

explore a huge mythological sandbox
get humbled by enemies that look way cooler than you
learn systems that actually make you think
occasionally lose progress and laugh/cry about it

10/10
a deep, quirky, challenging cultivation RPG that can be incredibly fun if you enjoy sandbox freedom and don’t mind a bit of a learning curve

short_description: Welcome to CREO, the megacorporation saving the world! A catastrophic event has knocked you out during the first day on the job… you wake up equipped with a heavy-grade exoskeleton, in a destroyed section of the complex.
detailed_description_raw:
A catastrophic event has knocked you out during the first day on the job… you wake up equipped with a heavy-grade exoskeleton, in a destroyed section of the complex. Robots gone haywire, insane augmented co-workers and rogue AI - everything wants you dead.

Defy deadly enemies and huge bosses in tight, visceral melee combat. Target and slice specific limbs off your foes, with a next-gen loot system where you loot what you dismember. Equip, upgrade and craft new weapons and armors sliced from enemies, and make yourself stronger through a fresh take on leveling-up.

['cool idea but absolutely terrible map design you will waste so much time going through every path possible to find out it leads right back to where you started and have made no progress or clue as to where to go or what to do
I hope the sequel is better with this', 'cyberpunk dark souls', "I'm not a big souls-like player, I played Dark Souls and a bit of Dark Souls 2 but otherwise I'm not really that into the genre. The Surge on the other hand really managed to pull my attention. I like the high tech aesthetic and the combat in which you face both human and robot enemies.

You play as Warren, a new employeefor CREO, a company focusing on engineering with their latest objective to restore the environment on earth through Project Resolve. Warrens interest mainly comes from getting access to an EXO rig, an exoskeleton that gives the user superhuman strength and endurance which would allow the crippled Warren to walk again.
However when his rig was surgically attached to him, the surgery robot malfunctions and forgets to sedate him which causes Warren to black out from the pain. When he reawakens he finds himself in a destroyed part of the facility, the worker drones and robots are malfunctioning and the workers all have their brain implant fried which put them into a zombified state, mindlessy wandering around and attacking Warren if he comes to close.

You make your way back to the main facility and try to find out what happened and how you can fix this. As you progress through the company you wil have to fight through many enemies that see you as a safety hazard. Luckily your exo rig allows you to carry heavy industry equipment that deals a ton of damage to enemies.
The combat in this game feels slow and heavy. There are faster weapons in the game but most of them are designed to have a slow windup time but high impact on enemies. This puts player into a difficult spot in which they have to time their attacks right to counter enemy attacks. Hits can stagger enemies which is easier on heavier weapons but takes more hits on lighter ones. Staggered enemies can be dealed with easier, which allows you to chain attack combos together. The Surge does not differentiate between heavy and light attacks and instead uses horizontal and vertical hits which players can use to create their own combos to stagger enemies and hit body parts they desire. Which is brings me to a focus point of the game, body parts. All enemies, both humanoid and robotic, have different parts players can target. Armored parts take less damage and take more impact to stagger. However, if you deal enough damage to armored body parts, you can cut it off through a finisher (for human enemies) or destroy it to reduce the danger of a robotic enemy. This also applies to bosses which have many different parts that can be detached or broken to gain a combat advantage. 
More importantely, this is also how you gain new gear. Because all human enemies wear the same rig as you, you can cut off their limbs to get that specific part from them. This allows you to craft the same armor for that body part in the base. The first time you do this for any body part you get the required schematic, further body parts will give you crafting materials to craft and upgrade those armor parts. This gives players direct control on what armor they want to farm from enemies. Not finishing enemies with an execution prevents you from gaining any materials because they destruct on death. You stil get scrap however which is used as the currency of this game. There are no shops in the game but you can use scrap to upgrade your rig which increases the amount of power it has. Power is required to equip the various armor pieces, higher tiers require more power. It also is used to equip various implants that you can find throughout the game by exploring the world. These implants have either an active or passive effect. Active effects can be something like healing or a buff, they are essentially your flask in the game. 
Your power is also used to open various doors in the game by shortcircuit exposed cables. This is in some cases required to progres sthrough the main game in most cases it is however used to open optional sections for extra loot.

The game lacks any traditional leveling, your gear is what defines your stats and the only way to get better stats is to farm enemies for crafting materials so you can upgrade it. This is however limited because enemies, just like your armor, come in tiers and you can only upgrade your armor to tier 2 if you can find and kill enemies of tier 2. This means that players will hit a wall and cannot powerlevel their way through the game is they are stuck. This can be somewhat offputting for experienced soul players but I personally like this system because it keeps things balanced and challenging.

But what about weapons? Enemies drop their weapons if you cut off the arm that holds the weapon. There are several weapon types in the game, one handed, two handed, staves, single- and two-rigged. Warrens gains profiency while using weapons of a specfic type which increases the base damage of that weapon. You still can upgrade the weapon just like armor, but this gives players at least some sort of standard leveleling progression even if the impact isn't as high as in other games. Each weapon has their own scaling efficiency, some weapons have higehr base damage but gain less from proficiency, others have low base damage but scale really well into thje late game. There are a lot of weapons and besides their csclaing power they also have different impact to stagger enemies as well as attack speed. Weapons also define energy gain which is used to execute enemies or activate several implants. This includes your personal drone (yes, you get one too) which helps you in combat.
There are also different damage types for weapons like elemental and slashing damage and enemies can have different resistances to various types but personally I never paid much attention. You don't really need to min-.max in this game to squeeze out more damage for bosses.

Speaking of bosses, this is one of the worse aspects of the game. The bosses are interesting because they are big robots (and in one case an entire assembly line), but there are only five different bosses in the base game to face. There are some minibosses you can find but that's it. Also during bosses the drawbacks of the combat system begin to shine. Combat feels cunkly, mainly because of the long windup time for attacks. Bosses are mostly immune to impact, so you cannot stagger them. The combat was clearly designed around the personal onne on one encounters against humanid enemies because it can become hard to hit enemies, you just clip through or get hit by something that didn't physically connect with your character. This can make bossfights frustrating. Then there is the issue with so called hardcore kills. Bosses drop their specific boss weapon on death but what the game does not tell you is that enemies can also drop an improved 2.0 version of the weapon that is stronger and has extra effects. You only get these by beating the boss in avery specific way and th egame gives you no clues how to do that or that it's even possible. I suggest to check out guides how to get those improved weapons because they are worth it.

Combat feels the best when battling single enemies and gets out of hand when you have to face entire groups, which will happen more and more as you progress through the game. I like the personal stype in combat, focusing on a single enemy and I think that's one of the games biggest strengths. Luckily you can bait enemies out of groups by using your drone, which was probably the intended purpose. 

I also want to praise the level design. Each section of the game is centered around a base that you can use to heal and craft and you will open shortcuts to always get back to that central point. This looping structure of th egame world makes navigating easier although the game could use a map in my opinion.", 'One of the worst games for combat ever made. It does not matter what DPS you have, there is always a delay when you click, the enemy will always hit first as they have no delay and they can cancel your attacks just so they can hit you, you can literally connect with them on your animation and they will cancel your attack and hit you while they are doing a slow animation. This game is a pile of s**t. There is also no map, you will have to go and look at maps on walls that are barely legible and because not a single place in this game is memorable even with the forced backtrack. Enemies and bosses have infinite health at random times, you will have no idea when you can attack them and then they will one-shot you. Regardless, it is still somewhat enjoyable.', 'scifi dark souls', 'its good just try it', 'GG', 'Got this in a sale, i used to enjoy this game on PS3. Now i get to enjoy in on Steam ;)', 'Why no one told me before about how fun this game is?!', 'If you’re looking for a Soulslike that brings something genuinely fresh to the table, **The Surge** is a must-play. While many games try to mimic the "FromSoftware" formula, Deck13 successfully carved out its own identity with a gritty, industrial sci-fi aesthetic and some of the most satisfying combat mechanics in the genre.

Why It’s Worth Your Time:

* The Targeted Limp-Slicing System: This is the game’s "killer app." Instead of just hacking at a health bar, you target specific limbs to tear away armor and tech. If you want a specific piece of gear, you cut it off the enemy. It turns every encounter into a tactical decision: do you hit the unarmored head for a quick kill, or the armored arm to loot that shiny new chainsaw?

* Deep Customization: The "Exo-Rig" and implant system allow for incredible build flexibility. You aren't locked into rigid stats; you can swap implants at Med-Bays to shift from a tanky powerhouse to a high-energy glass cannon depending on the boss you're facing.

* Atmosphere and Level Design: CREO is a labyrinthine, claustrophobic industrial nightmare. The way the levels loop back on themselves with clever shortcuts is top-tier. It captures that "I’m lost—oh wait, I’m back at the beginning!" feeling perfectly.

* Satisfying Weight: Every swing of a heavy duty wrench or twin-rigged blade feels impactful. The finishing animations are brutal, stylish, and never get old.

Final Verdict:
It’s tough, it’s fair, and it rewards patience and precision. If you’re tired of medieval fantasy and want to feel like a high-tech gladiator in a corporate dystopia, The Surge is an absolute gem. 

8.5/10 — Cut off their limbs and make them your own.', 'I have played souls-like games before, wanted to try out this one and so I did but ended quickly.
Tutorial boss is just random sponge-mechanic, that either spams the attacks all the time or when you hit him, it does absolutely nothing. I know the tactics, I literally had to check the video guides and some reddit posts how to kill him - just be behind him and attack/dodge and repeat until his armor or whatever is it drops.. well, I dont know why, but when I was behind him, he just automatically turned right at me like instantly at zero second but he moves much slower -> see the point? Theres no logic behind his animation. And if you even manage to somehow break him, the armor doesnt go away.
I just spent 15 tries to get him, only 2 times got him kneeled and was able to hit his health bar, but what was the damage I was dealing? Zero to one damage as to put it easily in words - and I have to repeat the same pain tactic all over again multiple times?

I can agree I may suck to this game especially, but just dont tell me a tutorial boss is a boss who determines what player may continue or may not.

Gameplay-wise this game is okay, but the punches you give with your weapons to enemies feels terrible, clunky, not very ,,punchy,, ... and souls-like games should always be about the feel when you punch an enemy.

Also there is no difficulty, so you are not able to even decrease the difficulty when you clearly doesnt have a chance of winning against TUTORIAL boss.

Yeah, funny isnt it.', 'The visuals looked great but the character movement and gameplay just felt junky and their difficulty curve was all over the place. Not to mention, their environments were very tough to navigate without some serious practice.', 'This game is broken', 'steamdeck review 
plays well on the deck 

good game challenging to play 

id recommend giving it a try ill up date more when i play more', 'Did you know, there was a SURGE! in vbuck prices! if you want to know more please search Fortnite Inflation!', "[REVIEW AFTER 100% COMPLETION]

Bought this game on sale for really cheap, didn't expect it to hook me like that. 
I enjoyed the storyline, it gets darker and more atmospheric every chapter. 

Level design is top tier for me, maps are large (you will get lost many times) but there are many shortcuts that you gonna find on every map. You need to pay attention where you going and memorize every corner because there is no map in this game. 

I liked combat system, its very unique, in order to obtain weapons or armor pieces you have to focus and cut it from your enemies that you come across, very satisfying.

Game can be demanding, but you can play it slowly and make your character more powerful by just farming and upgrade the core. 

I also recommend buying augmented edition where you get all dlcs, 2 of them are smaller stories that will add around 10 hours of gameplay. I enjoyed them too. 


If you going for 100% completion, it will take more than 1 playthrough, but ng+ can be speedrunned fast. 

Overall i had a lot of fun in this game and its a hidden gem for me, game is not popular and not that new, but give it a try, its almost free when on sale so...", "First playthrough review time.

This game has good gameplay, tough combat, and a world that looks interesting. The game might be challenging, and may be suited for players who prefer difficult experiences.

You might like this game if you want a tough, single-player game where you have to learn from mistakes. There is no multiplayer, and the game and the game provides limited guidance. Players need to solve the challenges on their own, without any in-game guidance. The game takes place in a dark, futuristic world. The game also has DLCs for extra gameplay content, and in those, I felt the 'A Walk in the Park' DLC is the best for its unique storyline.

', 'gg', 'Off with their limbs!', 'Meh', "To be honest I did not enjoy playing this game as much as other souls-likes I played over the last few years. I've hardly seen everything it has to offer, but I really felt like stopping right after beating the final boss. I still wanted to review it though, if only to highlight what I did like.

What I liked:
- The horror atmosphere. It starts off with a feeling close to a zombie game's, with all humans being in a sort of brain-dead-kill-everything-in-sight state when you wake up, and it evolves into something closer to cosmic horror by the end.
- Very good world-building and interesting story. The different areas of CREO all tell their stories thanks to an amazing amount of details in the environment, and you get to find a lot of logs that give you insight about what other people were doing before everything went wrong. I liked how the game introduces some key characters like Jonah or Don via in-game corporate advertisements.
- Excellent level-design, with each area being connected to others in a believable way, and built around a single rest location and a lot of unlockable shortcuts. And unlocking those felt amazing every time, as it really meant being able to skip long enemy cleanup segments.
- You can definitely beat a fair amount of bosses without taking a hit, although it is hard. And as soon as you know how to inflict damage to them, the fight can be over pretty fast. So a lot of them are like puzzles to solve, rather than endurance tests. They can be infuriating though.
- You can store remaining tech scrap that you cannot spend on leveling-up. So even a little can stack over a couple tries against enemy groups, and you can make progress all the time.

What I disliked:
- Combat and movement in general. It unfortunately did not click for me. It felt clunky, with unpleasant feedback (even if you target weak spots, you very often hit metallic parts and it does not feel good). My inputs often felt unresponsive, with too long transitions between attack animations, and a lot of inertia, which took some time to get used to. You need to learn the combos for all the types of weapons you find, and get used to the fact that some attacks trigger two hits instead of one. I imagine the skill ceiling of the game might be pretty high still, as you are encouraged to mix vertical and horizontal hits to make sure that you always hit the body part you are targeting while the enemies are doing their own thing.
- Although the idea of targeting specific limbs to collect upgrade materials is great in itself, as you do not have to hope RNG is generous to get new weapons or armor parts, it was not really fun for me to do. I grew tired of the slow-down dismembering kill animations pretty quickly.
- Some enemies, especially non-humanoïd ones, have hard to read attack animations, where you need to learn a timing instead of visual flow. Whenever such an attack empties your health bar, it feels devastating.
- Even if you open well-placed shortcuts, the runback to most bosses feels unnecessarily long, especially if you have to go through narrow corridors where an enemy cannot be avoided.

If you've played souls-like games before and really want to try one set on a sci-fi stage, it's not that bad, and it has its merits (world-building, level-design, story).
